content: Atoms in solids are in close contact, with forces between them that allow the atoms to vibrate but not to change positions with neighboring atoms. A solid resists all types of stress. In contrast, liquids deform easily when stressed and do not spring back to their original shape once the force is removed because the atoms are free to slide about and change neighbors—that is, they flow. Atoms in gases are separated by considerable distances, causing the forces between them to be weak. Gases and plasmas flow easily because their low density allows for easy compression.
summary: Description of atomic arrangements in solids, liquids, and gases.

content: Density is an important characteristic of substances, crucial in determining whether an object sinks or floats in a fluid. Density is defined as mass per unit volume. The SI unit of density is kg/m³. The density of an object may help identify its composition and gives insight into the phase of matter it represents.
description: Figure 11.3 illustrates the riddle comparing the weight of feathers versus bricks.
summary: Definition and significance of density in fluids.

content: To find the mass of water held in a reservoir, we calculate the volume using surface area and depth, then apply the density of water to get mass. The weight of the water is substantial, yet the dam does not need to entirely counteract this weight.
description: Figure 11.4 shows the Three Gorges Dam, illustrating large reservoir capacities.
summary: Calculating mass and weight of water in a reservoir.

content: Pressure is defined as the force applied to an area that is perpendicular to that force. The pressure exerted by fluids is defined for solids, liquids, and gases and is crucial to understanding fluid behavior.
description: Figure 11.5 shows how the same force can have different effects based on the area it is applied to.
summary: Definition of pressure and its significance in fluids.

content: Pressure increases with depth in a fluid due to the weight of the fluid above. The relationship between pressure and depth helps explain phenomena like ear popping during ascent or descent.
description: Figure 11.8 depicts how the bottom of a container supports the fluid's weight.
summary: Explanation of how pressure varies with depth in fluids.

content: Pascal’s principle states that a change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished throughout the fluid. This principle is vital in hydraulic systems.
description: Figure 11.11 illustrates a typical hydraulic system.
summary: Overview of Pascal's principle and its applications.

content: Gauge pressure measures pressure relative to atmospheric pressure, while absolute pressure includes atmospheric pressure. Devices like tire gauges measure pressure in this way.
description: Figure 11.13 shows a mechanical aneroid pressure gauge.
summary: Differentiation between gauge pressure and absolute pressure.

content: Archimedes’ principle states that the buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces. This concept is crucial for understanding why some objects float while others sink.
description: Figure 11.17 illustrates buoyant forces acting on submerged objects.
summary: Definition and implications of Archimedes' principle regarding buoyancy.

content: Cohesive and adhesive forces govern the behavior of liquids. Surface tension results from cohesive forces, affecting how liquids behave in various environments.
description: Figure 11.24 shows soap bubbles demonstrating cohesive forces.
summary: Overview of cohesive and adhesive forces and their effects on liquids.

header: Fluid Statics: Surface Tension and Its Effects
content: Rather, its weight is supported by forces in the stretched surface that try to make the surface smaller or flatter. If the needle were placed point down on the surface, its weight acting on a smaller area would break the surface, and it would sink.
summary: Surface tension supports objects on a liquid's surface without sinking.

header: Fluid Statics: Surface Tension and Its Effects (Figure 11.25)
content: FIGURE 11.25 Surface tension supporting the weight of an insect and an iron needle, both of which rest on the surface without penetrating it. They are not floating; rather, they are supported by the surface of the liquid. (a) An insect leg dents the water surface. is a restoring force (surface tension) parallel to the surface. (b) An iron needle similarly dents a water surface until the restoring force (surface tension) grows to equal its weight.
description: Illustration showing an insect and an iron needle on the surface of water, demonstrating surface tension.
summary: The figure illustrates how surface tension supports both an insect and an iron needle on a liquid surface.

header: Fluid Statics: Surface Tension Equation and Cohesive Forces
content: Surface tension is proportional to the strength of the cohesive force, which varies with the type of liquid. Surface tension is defined to be the force F per unit length exerted by a stretched liquid membrane: Table 11.3 lists values of for some liquids. For the insect of Figure 11.25(a), its weight is supported by the upward components of the surface tension force: , where is the circumference of the insect’s foot in contact with the water.
description: Table 11.3 lists values of surface tension for various liquids.
summary: Surface tension is influenced by cohesive forces and varies across different liquids.

content: Figure 11.26 shows one way to measure surface tension. The liquid film exerts a force on the movable wire in an attempt to reduce its surface area. The magnitude of this force depends on the surface tension of the liquid and can be measured accurately.
description: Diagram illustrating a device used to measure surface tension through the force exerted on a movable wire.
summary: This figure demonstrates a method of measuring surface tension in liquids.

header: Fluid Statics: Pressure Inside a Bubble
content: Surface tension is the reason why liquids form bubbles and droplets. The inward surface tension force causes bubbles to be approximately spherical and raises the pressure of the gas trapped inside relative to atmospheric pressure outside. It can be shown that the gauge pressure inside a spherical bubble is given by where is the radius of the bubble. Thus the pressure inside a bubble is greatest when the bubble is the smallest.
summary: Surface tension creates gauge pressure in bubbles, which is greater in smaller bubbles.

header: Fluid Statics: Balancing Pressure in Balloons (Figure 11.27)
content: Another bit of evidence for this is illustrated in Figure 11.27. When air is allowed to flow between two balloons of unequal size, the smaller balloon tends to collapse, filling the larger balloon.
description: Showing the behavior of air pressure in two different-sized balloons connected by a tube.
summary: This figure illustrates how air pressure behaves between balloons of different sizes.

header: Fluid Statics: Values of Surface Tension (Table 11.3)
content:
Liquid Surface tension γ(N/m)
Water at 0.0756
Water at 0.0728
Water at 0.0589
Soapy water (typical) 0.0370
Ethyl alcohol 0.0223
Glycerin 0.0631
Mercury 0.465
description: This table provides the surface tension values for various liquids at a standard temperature.
summary: Table 11.3 lists surface tension values for common liquids.

header: Fluid Statics: Example Problem on Surface Tension
content: EXAMPLE 11.11 Surface Tension: Pressure Inside a Bubble Calculate the gauge pressure inside a soap bubble in radius using the surface tension for soapy water in Table 11.3. Convert this pressure to mm Hg. Strategy The radius is given and the surface tension can be found in Table 11.3, and so can be found directly from the equation Solution Substituting and into the equation , we obtain. We use a conversion factor to get this into units of mm Hg.
summary: An example calculation determining gauge pressure in a soap bubble.

header: Fluid Statics: Discussion on Alveoli Pressures
content: Discussion Note that if a hole were to be made in the bubble, the air would be forced out, the bubble would decrease in radius, and the gauge pressure would reduce to zero, and the absolute pressure inside would decrease to atmospheric pressure (760 mm Hg). Our lungs contain hundreds of millions of mucus-lined sacs called alveoli, which are very similar in size, and about 0.1 mm in diameter.
summary: Explanation of how alveoli in the lungs function similarly to bubbles regarding pressure.

header: Fluid Statics: Air Pressure and Lung Function (Figure 11.28)
content: (See Figure 11.28.) You can exhale without muscle action by allowing surface tension to contract these sacs. Medical patients whose breathing is aided by a positive pressure respirator have air blown into the lungs, but are generally allowed to exhale on their own. Even if there is paralysis, surface tension in the alveoli will expel air from the lungs. Since pressure increases as the radii of the alveoli decrease, an occasional deep cleansing breath is needed to fully reinflate the alveoli. Respirators are programmed to do this and we find it natural, as do our companion dogs and cats, to take a cleansing breath before settling into a nap.
description: Illustration of the bronchial tubes in the lungs and how the presence of alveoli aids in lung function.
summary: This figure illustrates the structure of lungs and the role of alveoli in breathing.

header: Fluid Statics: Surface Tension in Alveoli
content: The tension in the walls of the alveoli results from the membrane tissue and a liquid on the walls of the alveoli containing a long lipoprotein that acts as a surfactant (a surface-tension reducing substance). The need for the surfactant results from the tendency of small alveoli to collapse and the air to fill into the larger alveoli making them even larger (as demonstrated in Figure 11.27).
summary: Surfactants help prevent alveoli from collapsing and facilitate breathing.

header: Fluid Statics: Role of Surfactants in the Lungs (Figure 11.29)
content: During inhalation, the lipoprotein molecules are pulled apart and the wall tension increases as the radius increases (increased surface tension). During exhalation, the molecules slide back together and the surface tension decreases, helping to prevent a collapse of the alveoli. The surfactant therefore serves to change the wall tension so that small alveoli don’t collapse and large alveoli are prevented from expanding too much. This tension change is a unique property of these surfactants, and is not shared by detergents (which simply lower surface tension). (See Figure 11.29.)
description: Figure shows the relationship between surface tension and surface area in lung surfactants.
summary: This figure illustrates how surfactants adjust surface tension in alveoli.

header: Fluid Statics: Impact of Water in Lungs
content: If water gets into the lungs, the surface tension is too great and you cannot inhale. This is a severe problem in resuscitating drowning victims. A similar problem occurs in newborn infants who are born without this surfactant—their lungs are very difficult to inflate. This condition is known as hyaline membrane disease and is a leading cause of death for infants, particularly in premature births. Some success has been achieved in treating hyaline membrane disease by spraying a surfactant into the infant’s breathing passages.
summary: Surfactant deficiency in newborns can lead to severe respiratory issues.

header: Fluid Statics: Emphysema and Lung Function
content: Emphysema produces the opposite problem with alveoli. Alveolar walls of emphysema victims deteriorate, and the sacs combine to form larger sacs. Because pressure produced by surface tension decreases with increasing radius, these larger sacs produce smaller pressure, reducing the ability of emphysema victims to exhale. A common test for emphysema is to measure the pressure and volume of air that can be exhaled.
summary: Emphysema affects lung function by altering alveolar pressure dynamics.

header: Fluid Statics: Investigative Actions Related to Surface Tension
content: Making Connections: Take-Home Investigation (1) Try floating a sewing needle on water. In order for this activity to work, the needle needs to be very clean as even the oil from your fingers can be sufficient to affect the surface properties of the needle. (2) Place the bristles of a paint brush into water. Pull the brush out and notice that for a short while, the bristles will stick together. The surface tension of the water surrounding the bristles is sufficient to hold the bristles together. As the bristles dry out, the surface tension effect dissipates. (3) Place a loop of thread on the surface of still water in such a way that all of the thread is in contact with the water. Note the shape of the loop. Now place a drop of detergent into the middle of the loop. What happens to the shape of the loop? Why? (4) Sprinkle pepper onto the surface of water. Add a drop of detergent. What happens? Why? (5) Float two matches parallel to each other and add a drop of detergent between them. What happens? Note: For each new experiment, the water needs to be replaced and the bowl washed to free it of any residual detergent.
summary: A series of home experiments to explore the effects of surface tension.

header: Fluid Statics: Adhesion and Capillary Action - Concept Introduction
content: Adhesion and Capillary Action Why is it that water beads up on a waxed car but does not on bare paint? The answer is that the adhesive forces between water and wax are much smaller than those between water and paint. Competition between the forces of adhesion and cohesion are important in the macroscopic behavior of liquids.
summary: The differences in adhesion and cohesion forces affect how liquids behave on surfaces.

header: Fluid Statics: The Contact Angle of Liquids
content: An important factor in studying the roles of these two forces is the angle between the tangent to the liquid surface and the surface. (See Figure 11.30.) The contact angle is directly related to the relative strength of the cohesive and adhesive forces. The larger the strength of the cohesive force relative to the adhesive force, the larger the contact angle, and the more the liquid tends to form a droplet.
description: Figure 11.30 illustrates how contact angles vary with surface interactions.
summary: The contact angle is a crucial indicator of the balance between adhesive and cohesive forces in liquids.

header: Fluid Statics: Capillary Action and Its Definition
content: Capillary Action The tendency of a fluid to be raised or suppressed in a narrow tube, or capillary tube, is called capillary action.
summary: Capillary action describes how liquids behave in confined spaces.

header: Fluid Statics: Capillary Action in Tubes (Figure 11.31)
content: If a capillary tube is placed vertically into a liquid, as shown in Figure 11.31, capillary action will raise or suppress the liquid inside the tube depending on the combination of substances. The actual effect depends on the relative strength of the cohesive and adhesive forces and, thus, the contact angle given in the table. If the contact angle is less than 90 degrees, the fluid will be raised; if it is greater than 90 degrees, it will be suppressed.
description: Figure showing the effect of a capillary tube on fluid behavior, highlighting adhesion and cohesion.
summary: This figure illustrates how liquid behaves in a capillary tube based on cohesive and adhesive forces.

header: Fluid Statics: Mercury Behavior in Tubes
content: Mercury, for example, has a very large surface tension and a large contact angle with glass. When placed in a tube, the surface of a column of mercury curves downward, somewhat like a drop. The curved surface of a fluid in a tube is called a meniscus. The tendency of surface tension is always to reduce the surface area. Surface tension thus flattens the curved liquid surface in a capillary tube. This results in a downward force in mercury and an upward force in water, as seen in Figure 11.31.
summary: The behavior of mercury in tubes exemplifies the principles of surface tension and meniscus formation.

header: Fluid Statics: Height of Fluid in Capillary Action
content: Capillary action can move liquids horizontally over very large distances, but the height to which it can raise or suppress a liquid in a tube is limited by its weight. It can be shown that this height is given by the formula h = (2γ cos θ) / (ρgR), where h is the height, γ is the surface tension, ρ is the fluid density, g is the gravitational constant, and R is the radius of the tube.
summary: The height a liquid can rise in a capillary tube is determined by its surface tension and density.

header: Fluid Statics: Example Calculation of Fluid Height
content: EXAMPLE 11.12 Calculating Radius of a Capillary Tube: Capillary Action: Tree Sap Can capillary action be solely responsible for sap rising in trees? To answer this question, calculate the radius of a capillary tube that would raise sap 100 m to the top of a giant redwood, assuming that sap’s density is known, its contact angle is zero, and its surface tension is the same as that of water.
summary: An example demonstrating the calculation of capillary action in trees.

header: Fluid Statics: Discussion on Tree Sap Movement
content: Discussion This result is unreasonable. Sap in trees moves through the xylem, which forms tubes with radii as small as several micrometers. This value is about 180 times as large as the radius found necessary here to raise sap 100 m. This means that capillary action alone cannot be solely responsible for sap getting to the tops of trees. How does sap get to the tops of tall trees? (Recall that a column of water can only rise to a height of 10 m when there is a vacuum at the top—see Example 11.5.)
summary: This paragraph discusses the insufficiency of capillary action in moving tree sap to great heights.

header: Fluid Statics: Mechanism of Sap Movement - Cohesive Forces
content: The question has not been completely resolved, but it appears that it is pulled up like a chain held together by cohesive forces. As each molecule of sap enters a leaf and evaporates (a process called transpiration), the entire chain is pulled up a notch. So a negative pressure created by water evaporation must be present to pull the sap up through the xylem vessels.
summary: Cohesive forces and transpiration are involved in how sap moves through trees.

header: Fluid Statics: Negative Pressure Device for Sap
content: In most situations, fluids can push but can exert only negligible pull because the cohesive forces seem to be too small to hold the molecules tightly together. But in this case, the cohesive force of water molecules provides a very strong pull. Figure 11.33 shows one device for studying negative pressure.
description: Figure illustrating a device designed for studying negative pressure in liquids.
summary: This figure shows a device designed to investigate negative pressure conditions in liquids.

header: Fluid Statics: Measuring Pressures in the Body
content: 11.9 Pressures in the Body LEARNING OBJECTIVES By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Explain the concept of pressure in the human body. • Explain systolic and diastolic blood pressures. • Describe pressures in the eye, lungs, spinal column, bladder, and skeletal system.
summary: Overview of the various pressures found in the human body.

header: Fluid Statics: Measuring Blood Pressure and Its Importance
content: Pressure in the Body Next to taking a person’s temperature and weight, measuring blood pressure is the most common of all medical examinations. Control of high blood pressure is largely responsible for the significant decreases in heart attack and stroke fatalities achieved in the last three decades. The pressures in various parts of the body can be measured and often provide valuable medical indicators. In this section, we consider a few examples together with some of the physics that accompanies them.
summary: Blood pressure measurements are crucial for diagnosing and managing cardiovascular health.

header: Fluid Statics: Typical Pressures in the Body (Table 11.5)
content: Table 11.5 lists some of the measured pressures in mm Hg, the units most commonly quoted. Body system Gauge pressure in mm Hg Blood pressures in large arteries (resting) Maximum (systolic) 100–140 Minimum (diastolic) 60–90 Blood pressure in large veins 4–15 Eye 12–24 Brain and spinal fluid (lying down) 5–12 Bladder While filling 0–25 When full 100–150 Chest cavity between lungs and ribs −8 to −4 Inside lungs −2 to +3 Digestive tract Esophagus −2 Stomach 0–20 Intestines 10–20 Middle ear <1.
description: This table summarizes typical pressure values in various body systems.
summary: Table 11.5 provides an overview of typical pressures measured in different parts of the human body.

header: Fluid Statics: Systolic and Diastolic Pressures Explained
content: Blood Pressure Common arterial blood pressure measurements typically produce values of 120 mm Hg and 80 mm Hg, respectively, for systolic and diastolic pressures. Both pressures have health implications. When systolic pressure is chronically high, the risk of stroke and heart attack is increased. If, however, it is too low, fainting is a problem. Systolic pressure increases dramatically during exercise to increase blood flow and returns to normal afterward. This change produces no ill effects and, in fact, may be beneficial to the tone of the circulatory system.
summary: Systolic and diastolic pressures are critical indicators of cardiovascular health.

header: Fluid Statics: Implications of Blood Pressure
content: Diastolic pressure can be an indicator of fluid balance. When low, it may indicate that a person is hemorrhaging internally and needs a transfusion. Conversely, high diastolic pressure indicates a ballooning of the blood vessels, which may be due to the transfusion of too much fluid into the circulatory system. High diastolic pressure is also an indication that blood vessels are not dilating properly to pass blood through. This can seriously strain the heart in its attempt to pump blood.
summary: Changes in diastolic pressure signal potential issues related to fluid balance and vascular health.

header: Fluid Statics: Blood Pressure Dynamics Through the Body
content: Blood leaves the heart at about 120 mm Hg but its pressure continues to decrease (to almost 0) as it goes from the aorta to smaller arteries to small veins (see Figure 11.34). The pressure differences in the circulation system are caused by blood flow through the system as well as the position of the person. For a person standing up, the pressure in the feet will be larger than at the heart due to the weight of the blood.
description: Illustration of blood pressure dynamics as blood travels through arteries and veins.
summary: Blood pressure decreases progressively from the heart through the circulatory system.

header: Fluid Statics: Calculating Pressure Increase in Upright Position
content: If we assume that the distance between the heart and the feet of a person in an upright position is 1.4 m, then the increase in pressure in the feet relative to that in the heart (for a static column of blood) is given by the formula P = ρgh.
summary: This section discusses pressure increase in the feet of an upright person due to blood weight.

header: Fluid Statics: Pressure and Blood Accumulation in the Legs
content: Standing a long time can lead to an accumulation of blood in the legs and swelling. This is the reason why soldiers who are required to stand still for long periods of time have been known to faint. Elastic bandages around the calf can help prevent this accumulation and can also help provide increased pressure to enable the veins to send blood back up to the heart. For similar reasons, doctors recommend tight stockings for long-haul flights.
summary: Prolonged standing increases blood pressure in the legs, leading to swelling and fainting risks.

header: Fluid Statics: Invasive Blood Pressure Monitoring
content: Blood pressure may also be measured in the major veins, the heart chambers, arteries to the brain, and the lungs. But these pressures are usually only monitored during surgery or for patients in intensive care since the measurements are invasive. To obtain these pressure measurements, qualified health care workers thread thin tubes, called catheters, into appropriate locations to transmit pressures to external measuring devices.
summary: Invasive techniques are employed for precise blood pressure monitoring in clinical settings.

header: Fluid Statics: Dual Action of the Heart
content: The heart consists of two pumps—the right side forcing blood through the lungs and the left causing blood to flow through the rest of the body (Figure 11.34). Right-heart failure, for example, results in a rise in the pressure in the vena cavae and a drop in pressure in the arteries to the lungs. Left-heart failure results in a rise in the pressure entering the left side of the heart and a drop in aortal pressure.
description: Illustration showing the heart's structure and blood flow directions.
summary: The heart's dual pumping structure affects blood pressure in different vascular systems.

header: Fluid Statics: Effects of Pressure on Flow
content: Implications of these and other pressures on flow in the circulatory system will be discussed in more detail in Fluid Dynamics and Its Biological and Medical Applications.
summary: Understanding pressures is key to comprehending fluid dynamics in the human body.

header: Fluid Statics: Intraocular Pressure in the Eye
content: Pressure in the Eye The shape of the eye is maintained by fluid pressure, called intraocular pressure, which is normally in the range of 12.0 to 24.0 mm Hg. When the circulation of fluid in the eye is blocked, it can lead to a buildup in pressure, a condition called glaucoma. The net pressure can become as great as 85.0 mm Hg, an abnormally large pressure that can permanently damage the optic nerve.
summary: Intraocular pressure is crucial for maintaining eye shape and health.

header: Fluid Statics: Force Related to Eye Pressure
content: To get an idea of the force involved, suppose the back of the eye has an area of 1 cm² and the net pressure is 85.0 mm Hg. Force is given by F = P × A. To get F in newtons, we convert the area to m² (1 cm² = 0.0001 m²). Then we calculate F as follows:
summary: Calculating force based on intraocular pressure highlights potential damage risks.

header: Fluid Statics: Measures for Glaucoma Treatment
content: This pressure would equal the weight of about a 680-g mass. A mass of 680 g resting on the eye (imagine 1.5 lb resting on your eye) would be sufficient to cause it damage. (A normal force here would be the weight of about 120 g, less than one-quarter of our initial value.)
summary: Excessive eye pressure can cause severe damage, equivalent to the weight of a small object.

header: Fluid Statics: Routine Intraocular Pressure Checks
content: People over 40 years of age are at greatest risk of developing glaucoma and should have their intraocular pressure tested routinely. Most measurements involve exerting a force on the (anesthetized) eye over some area (a pressure) and observing the eye’s response. A noncontact approach uses a puff of air and a measurement is made of the force needed to indent the eye.
summary: Routine eye pressure checks are essential for early detection of glaucoma.

content: If the intraocular pressure is high, the eye will deform less and rebound more vigorously than normal.
description: Illustration of the tonometer used to measure intraocular pressure.
summary: This figure displays the tonometer, a device used for measuring intraocular pressure.

header: Fluid Statics: Eardrum Pressure and Damage
content: EXAMPLE 11.13 Calculating Gauge Pressure and Depth: Damage to the Eardrum Suppose a 3.00-N force can rupture an eardrum. (a) If the eardrum has an area of 2.00 cm², calculate the maximum tolerable gauge pressure on the eardrum in newtons per meter squared and convert it to millimeters of mercury.
summary: This example problem calculates the gauge pressure that can damage an eardrum.

header: Fluid Statics: Determining Pressure From Water Depth
content: (b) At what depth in freshwater would this person’s eardrum rupture, assuming the gauge pressure in the middle ear is zero?
summary: This part of the example estimates the water depth that could rupture an eardrum.

header: Fluid Statics: Lungs and Pressure Dynamics
content: Pressure Associated with the Lungs The pressure inside the lungs increases and decreases with each breath. The pressure drops to below atmospheric pressure (negative gauge pressure) when you inhale, causing air to flow into the lungs. It increases above atmospheric pressure (positive gauge pressure) when you exhale, forcing air out.
summary: Lung pressure is dynamic, changing with inhalation and exhalation.

header: Fluid Statics: Mechanisms of Lung Pressure Control
content: Lung pressure is controlled by several mechanisms. Muscle action in the diaphragm and rib cage is necessary for inhalation; this muscle action increases the volume of the lungs thereby reducing the pressure within them figure 11.36. Surface tension in the alveoli creates a positive pressure opposing inhalation. (See Cohesion and Adhesion in Liquids: Surface Tension and Capillary Action.) You can exhale without muscle action by letting surface tension in the alveoli create its own positive pressure.
description: Illustration showing the mechanics of lung function during inhalation and exhalation.
summary: Muscle action regulates lung pressure during breathing, while surface tension aids exhalation.

content: The lungs, in fact, would collapse due to the surface tension in the alveoli if they were not attached to the inside of the chest wall by liquid adhesion. The gauge pressure in the liquid attaching the lungs to the inside of the chest wall is thus negative, ranging from -8 to -4 during exhalation and inhalation, respectively. If air is allowed to enter the chest cavity, it breaks the attachment, and one or both lungs may collapse.
summary: Liquid adhesion prevents lung collapse by maintaining negative pressure in the chest cavity.

header: Fluid Statics: Surgical Interventions for Lung Inflation
content: Suction is applied to the chest cavity of surgery patients and trauma victims to reestablish negative pressure and inflate the lungs.
summary: Suction techniques are used in surgeries to restore lung function post-collapses.

header: Fluid Statics: Summary of Pressures in the Body
content: Other Pressures in the Body Spinal Column and Skull Normally, there is a 5- to 12-mm Hg pressure in the fluid surrounding the brain and filling the spinal column. This cerebrospinal fluid serves many purposes, one of which is to supply flotation to the brain. The buoyant force supplied by the fluid nearly equals the weight of the brain, since their densities are nearly equal. If there is a loss of fluid, the brain rests on the inside of the skull, causing severe headaches, constricted blood flow, and serious damage.
summary: Cerebrospinal fluid provides cushioning for the brain, vital for preventing damage.

header: Fluid Statics: Methods to Measure Cerebrospinal Pressure
content: Spinal fluid pressure is measured by means of a needle inserted between vertebrae that transmits the pressure to a suitable measuring device.
summary: Pressure in the spinal column is gauged through invasive techniques.

content: Bladder Pressure This bodily pressure is one of which we are often aware. In fact, there is a relationship between our awareness of this pressure and a subsequent increase in it. Bladder pressure climbs steadily from zero to about 25 mm Hg as the bladder fills to its normal capacity of 300 ml. This pressure triggers the micturition reflex, which stimulates the feeling of needing to urinate.
summary: Bladder pressure correlates with the urge to urinate as the bladder fills.

header: Fluid Statics: Increased Bladder Pressure Effects
content: What is more, it also causes muscles around the bladder to contract, raising the pressure to over 100 mm Hg, accentuating the sensation. Coughing, straining, tensing in cold weather, wearing tight clothes, and experiencing simple nervous tension all can increase bladder pressure and trigger this reflex. So can the weight of a pregnant woman’s fetus, especially if it is kicking vigorously or pushing down with its head!
summary: High bladder pressure is affected by various factors leading to increased urgency.

header: Fluid Statics: Measuring Bladder Pressure Techniques
content: Bladder pressure can be measured by a catheter or by inserting a needle through the bladder wall and transmitting the pressure to an appropriate measuring device. One hazard of high bladder pressure (sometimes created by an obstruction) is that such pressure can force urine back into the kidneys, causing potentially severe damage.
summary: Invasive methods are employed to measure bladder pressure, with high levels causing complications.

header: Fluid Statics: Intellectual Impact of Body Weight
content: Pressures in the Skeletal System These pressures are the largest in the body, due both to the high values of initial force and the small areas to which this force is applied, such as in the joints. For example, when a person lifts an object improperly, a force of 5000 N may be created between vertebrae in the spine, and this may be applied to an area as small as 0.002 m². The pressure created is 2.5 × 10⁶ Pa or about 50 atm! This pressure can damage both the spinal discs (the cartilage between vertebrae) and the bony vertebrae themselves.
summary: Skeletal pressures can reach harmful levels due to high forces on small areas.

header: Fluid Statics: Additional Body Pressure Examples
content: Even under normal circumstances, forces between vertebrae in the spine are large enough to create pressures of several atmospheres. Most causes of excessive pressure in the skeletal system can be avoided by lifting properly and avoiding extreme physical activity.
summary: Proper biomechanics are critical to prevent excessive pressures in the skeletal system.

header: Fluid Statics: Other Medical Pressures in the Body
content: There are many other interesting and medically significant pressures in the body. For example, pressure caused by various muscle actions drives food and waste through the digestive system. Stomach pressure behaves much like bladder pressure and is tied to the sensation of hunger. Pressure in the relaxed esophagus is normally negative because pressure in the chest cavity is normally negative. Positive pressure in the stomach may thus force acid into the esophagus, causing 'heartburn.'
summary: Various bodily pressures affect digestion and can lead to conditions like heartburn.

header: Fluid Statics: Middle Ear Pressure Dynamics
content: Pressure in the middle ear can result in significant force on the eardrum if it differs greatly from atmospheric pressure, such as while scuba diving. The decrease in external pressure is also noticeable during plane flights (due to a decrease in the weight of air above relative to that at the Earth’s surface). The Eustachian tubes connect the middle ear to the throat and allow us to equalize pressure in the middle ear to avoid an imbalance of force on the eardrum.
summary: Pressure variations can significantly impact the ears during altitude changes.

header: Fluid Statics: Conclusion on Body Pressures
content: Many pressures in the human body are associated with the flow of fluids. Fluid flow will be discussed in detail in the Fluid Dynamics and Its Biological and Medical Applications.
summary: Understanding body pressures is fundamental to exploring fluid dynamics and its medical relevance.

content: absolute pressure the sum of gauge pressure and atmospheric pressure adhesive forces the attractive forces between molecules of different types Archimedes’ principle the buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces buoyant force the net upward force on any object in any fluid bundled fluid a collection of fluids cohesive forces the attractive forces between molecules of the same type contact angle the angle between the tangent to liquid surface and the surface density the mass per unit volume of a substance or object specific gravity the ratio of the density of an object to a fluid (usually water) surface tension the cohesive forces between molecules which cause the surface of a liquid to contract to the smallest possible surface area
summary: A glossary defining essential terms related to fluid statics and dynamics.

header: Fluid Statics: Summary of Fluid Concepts
content: 11.1 What Is a Fluid? A fluid is a state of matter that yields to sideways or shearing forces. Liquids and gases are both fluids. Fluid statics is the physics of stationary fluids. 11.2 Density Density is the mass per unit volume of a substance or object. In equation form, density is defined as. The SI unit of density is kg/m³. 11.3 Pressure Pressure is the force per unit area. A change in pressure applied to an enclosed fluid is transmitted undiminished to all portions of the fluid and to the walls of its container.
summary: Basic principles of fluid statics, including fluid definition and density.

content: 11.4 Variation of Pressure with Depth in a Fluid Pressure is the weight of the fluid divided by the area supporting it (the area of the bottom of the container): 11.5 Pascal’s Principle Pressure is force per unit area. A hydraulic system is an enclosed fluid system used to exert forces. 11.6 Gauge Pressure, Absolute Pressure, and Pressure Measurement Gauge pressure is the pressure relative to atmospheric pressure. The SI unit of pressure is pascal (Pa). Absolute pressure is the sum of gauge pressure and atmospheric pressure.
summary: Definitions and principles related to pressure and hydraulics.

content: 11.7 Archimedes’ Principle Buoyant force is the net upward force on any object in any fluid. If the buoyant force is greater than the object’s weight, the object will rise to the surface and float. If the buoyant force is less than the object’s weight, the object will sink. If the buoyant force equals the object’s weight, the object will remain suspended at that depth. The buoyant force is always present whether the object floats, sinks, or is suspended in a fluid. Archimedes’ principle states that the buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces.
summary: Archimedes' principle explains buoyant forces and the behavior of objects in fluids.

header: Fluid Statics: Key Takeaways from Cohesion and Adhesion
content: 11.8 Cohesion and Adhesion in Liquids: Surface Tension and Capillary Action Attractive forces between molecules of the same type are called cohesive forces. Attractive forces between molecules of different types are called adhesive forces. Cohesive forces between molecules cause the surface of a liquid to contract to the smallest possible surface area. This general effect is called surface tension. Capillary action is the tendency of a fluid to be raised or suppressed in a narrow tube, or capillary tube, due to cohesion and adhesion.
summary: The interaction between cohesive and adhesive forces influences fluid behavior.

header: Fluid Statics: Summary of Pressures in the Body
content: 11.9 Pressures in the Body Measuring blood pressure is among the most common of all medical examinations. The pressures in various parts of the body can be measured and often provide valuable medical indicators. The shape of the eye is maintained by fluid pressure, called intraocular pressure. When the circulation of fluid in the eye is blocked, it can lead to a buildup in pressure, a condition called glaucoma.
summary: Blood pressure measurements are vital in evaluating health, and intraocular pressure affects vision.

header: Fluid Statics: Conceptual Questions about Fluids
content: Conceptual Questions 11.1 What Is a Fluid? 7. Figure 11.37 shows a glass of ice water filled to the brim. Will the water overflow when the ice melts? Explain your answer. 1. What physical characteristic distinguishes a fluid from a solid? 2. Which of the following substances are fluids at room temperature: air, mercury, water, glass?
summary: A list of conceptual questions aimed at understanding fluid properties and behaviors.

content: Heroes in movies hide beneath water and breathe through a hollow reed (villains never catch on to this trick). In practice, you cannot inhale in this manner if your lungs are more than 60.0 cm below the surface. What is the maximum negative gauge pressure you can create in your lungs on dry land, assuming you can achieve water pressure with your lungs 60.0 cm below the surface?
summary: Explores the concept of pressure and its limits in underwater breathing scenarios.

content: Calculate the gauge pressures inside 2.00-cm-radius bubbles of water, alcohol, and soapy water. Which liquid forms the most stable bubbles, neglecting any effects of evaporation?
summary: Calculates gauge pressure for different liquid bubbles.

content: Suppose water is raised by capillary action to a height of 5.00 cm in a glass tube. (a) To what height will it be raised in a paraffin tube of the same radius? (b) In a silver tube of the same radius?
summary: Analyzes how different materials affect the height of liquid in capillary tubes.

header: Contact Angle of Olive Oil in Capillary Action
content: Calculate the contact angle for olive oil if capillary action raises it to a height of 7.07 cm in a glass tube with a radius of 0.100 mm. Is this value consistent with that for most organic liquids?
summary: Explores the effects of contact angle in relation to capillary action.

content: When two soap bubbles touch, the larger is inflated by the smaller until they form a single bubble. (a) What is the gauge pressure inside a soap bubble with a 1.50-cm radius? (b) Inside a 4.00-cm-radius soap bubble? (c) Inside the single bubble they form if no air is lost when they touch?
summary: Investigates gauge pressure in different configurations of soap bubbles.

content: Calculate the ratio of the heights to which water and mercury are raised by capillary action in the same glass tube. What is the ratio of heights to which ethyl alcohol and water are raised by capillary action in the same glass tube?
summary: Examines the comparative capillary action of water, mercury, and ethyl alcohol.

content: Gauge pressure in the fluid surrounding an infant’s brain may rise as high as 85.0 mm Hg (5 to 12 mm Hg is normal), creating an outward force large enough to make the skull grow abnormally large. (a) Calculate this outward force in newtons on each side of an infant’s skull if the effective area of each side is . (b) What is the net force acting on the skull?
summary: Discusses the implications of elevated pressure on an infant's skull.

content: A full-term fetus typically has a mass of 3.50 kg. (a) What pressure does the weight of such a fetus create if it rests on the mother’s bladder, supported on an area of ? (b) Convert this pressure to millimeters of mercury and determine if it alone is great enough to trigger the micturition reflex (it will add to any pressure already existing in the bladder).
summary: Calculates the pressure exerted by a fetus on the mother's bladder.

content: If the pressure in the esophagus is while that in the stomach is , to what height could stomach fluid rise in the esophagus, assuming a density of 1.10 g/mL? (This movement will not occur if the muscle closing the lower end of the esophagus is working properly.)
summary: Analyzes pressure dynamics between the esophagus and stomach.

content: Pressure in the spinal fluid is measured as shown in Figure 11.40. If the pressure in the spinal fluid is 10.0 mm Hg: (a) What is the reading of the water manometer in cm water? (b) What is the reading if the person sits up, placing the top of the fluid 60 cm above the tap? The fluid density is 1.05 g/mL.
description: Figure 11.40 shows a water manometer used to measure pressure in the spinal fluid, with fluid height relative to the spinal column.
summary: Provides methods to measure spinal fluid pressure using a manometer.

content: Calculate the maximum force in newtons exerted by the blood on an aneurysm, or ballooning, in a major artery, given the maximum blood pressure for this person is 150 mm Hg and the effective area of the aneurysm is . Note that this force is great enough to cause further enlargement and subsequently greater force on the ever-thinner vessel wall.
summary: Explores the forces acting on an aneurysm due to blood pressure.

content: During heavy lifting, a disk between spinal vertebrae is subjected to a 5000-N compressional force. (a) What pressure is created, assuming that the disk has a uniform circular cross section 2.00 cm in radius? (b) What deformation is produced if the disk is 0.800 cm thick and has a Young’s modulus of ?
summary: Analyzes the effects of compressive forces on spinal disks.

header: Pressure Dynamics and Energy in Affected Individuals
content: When a person sits erect, increasing the vertical position of their brain by 36.0 cm, the heart must continue to pump blood to the brain at the same rate. (a) What is the gain in gravitational potential energy for 100 mL of blood raised 36.0 cm? (b) What is the drop in pressure, neglecting any losses due to friction? (c) Discuss how the gain in gravitational potential energy and the decrease in pressure are related.
summary: Links gravitational potential energy to blood pressure in an upright position.

content: (a) How high will water rise in a glass capillary tube with a 0.500-mm radius? (b) How much gravitational potential energy does the water gain? (c) Discuss possible sources of this energy.
summary: Explores the dynamics of water rise in a capillary tube.

content: The hydraulic system of a backhoe is used to lift a load as shown in Figure 11.42. (a) Calculate the force the secondary cylinder must exert to support the 400-kg load and the 150-kg brace and shovel. (b) What is the pressure in the hydraulic fluid if the secondary cylinder is 2.50 cm in diameter? (c) What force would you have to exert on a lever with a mechanical advantage of 5.00 acting on a primary cylinder 0.800 cm in diameter to create this pressure?
description: Figure 11.42 illustrates hydraulic and mechanical lever systems used in heavy machinery.
summary: Calculates the forces and pressures involved in hydraulic systems.

header: Pumping Bicycle Tires and Pressure Issues
content: You are pumping up a bicycle tire with a hand pump, the piston of which has a 2.00-cm radius. (a) What force in newtons must you exert to create a pressure of (b) What is unreasonable about this (a) result? (c) Which premises are unreasonable or inconsistent?
summary: Analyzes the mechanics behind pumping a bicycle tire.

content: Consider a group of people trying to stay afloat after their boat strikes a log in a lake. Construct a problem in which you calculate the number of people that can cling to the log and keep their heads out of the water. Among the variables to be considered are the size and density of the log, and what is needed to keep a person’s head and arms above water without swimming or treading water.
summary: Calculates how many people can float on a log without swimming.

content: The alveoli in emphysema victims are damaged and effectively form larger sacs. Construct a problem in which you calculate the loss of pressure due to surface tension in the alveoli because of their larger average diameters. (Part of the lung’s ability to expel air results from pressure created by surface tension in the alveoli.) Among the things to consider are the normal surface tension of the fluid lining the alveoli, the average alveolar radius in normal individuals and its average in emphysema sufferers.
summary: Examines the implications of surface tension effects on alveoli in emphysema.

header: Introduction to Fluid Dynamics and Its Biological and Medical Applications
content: We have dealt with many situations in which fluids are static. But by their very definition, fluids flow. Examples come easily—a column of smoke rises from a camp fire, water streams from a fire hose, blood courses through your veins. Why does rising smoke curl and twist? How does a nozzle increase the speed of water emerging from a hose? How does the body regulate blood flow? The physics of fluids in motion—fluid dynamics—allows us to answer these and many other questions.
summary: Fluid dynamics explores the behavior of flowing fluids and has various applications in biology and medicine.

header: Flow Rate and Its Relation to Velocity - Learning Objectives
content: By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Calculate flow rate. • Define units of volume. • Describe incompressible fluids. • Explain the consequences of the equation of continuity.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to flow rate and its relationship with velocity.

content: Flow rate is defined to be the volume of fluid passing by some location through an area during a period of time, as seen in Figure 12.2. In symbols, this can be written as where is the volume and is the elapsed time. The SI unit for flow rate is , but a number of other units for are in common use. For example, the heart of a resting adult pumps blood at a rate of 5.00 liters per minute (L/min). Note that a liter (L) is 1/1000 of a cubic meter or 1000 cubic centimeters ( or ). In this text, we shall use whatever metric units are most convenient for a given situation.
description: Figure 12.2 illustrates flow rate as the volume of fluid per unit time flowing past a point through a defined area.
summary: Flow rate quantifies the volume of fluid passing through a specific area over time, typically measured in liters per minute.

content:
EXAMPLE 12.1
Calculating Volume from Flow Rate: The Heart Pumps a Lot of Blood in a Lifetime
How many cubic meters of blood does the heart pump in a 75-year lifetime, assuming the average flow rate is 5.00 L/min? Strategy Time and flow rate are given, and so the volume can be calculated from the definition of flow rate. Solution Solving for volume gives. Substituting known values yields. Discussion This amount is about 200,000 tons of blood. For comparison, this value is equivalent to about 200 times the volume of water contained in a 6-lane 50-m lap pool.
summary: This example calculates the total volume of blood pumped by the heart over a lifetime based on flow rate.

header: Relationship Between Flow Rate and Velocity
content: Flow rate and velocity are related, but quite different, physical quantities. To make the distinction clear, think about the flow rate of a river. The greater the velocity of the water, the greater the flow rate of the river. But flow rate also depends on the size of the river. A rapid mountain stream carries far less water than the Amazon River in Brazil, for example. The precise relationship between flow rate and velocity is: where is the cross-sectional area and is the average velocity. This equation seems logical enough. The relationship tells us that flow rate is directly proportional to both the magnitude of the average velocity (hereafter referred to as the speed) and the size of a river, pipe, or other conduit.
summary: The flow rate of a fluid is influenced by its velocity and the cross-sectional area of the conduit.

content: Figure 12.3 shows an incompressible fluid flowing along a pipe of decreasing radius. Because the fluid is incompressible, the same amount of fluid must flow past any point in the tube in a given time to ensure continuity of flow. In this case, because the cross-sectional area of the pipe decreases, the velocity must necessarily increase. This logic can be extended to say that the flow rate must be the same at all points along the pipe. In particular, for points 1 and 2, this is called the equation of continuity and is valid for any incompressible fluid.
description: Figure 12.3 shows how the speed of a fluid increases as it flows through a narrowing pipe.
summary: An incompressible fluid must maintain constant flow rate, resulting in increased velocity in narrower sections of a pipe.

content: The consequences of the equation of continuity can be observed when water flows from a hose into a narrow spray nozzle: it emerges with a large speed—that is the purpose of the nozzle. Conversely, when a river empties into one end of a reservoir, the water slows considerably, perhaps picking up speed again when it leaves the other end of the reservoir. In other words, speed increases when cross-sectional area decreases, and speed decreases when cross-sectional area increases.
summary: The equation of continuity explains how fluid speed varies with changes in cross-sectional area in different scenarios.

header: Calculating Fluid Speed in a Hose and Nozzle
content:
EXAMPLE 12.2
Calculating Fluid Speed: Speed Increases When a Tube Narrows
A nozzle with a radius of 0.250 cm is attached to a garden hose with a radius of 0.900 cm. The flow rate through hose and nozzle is 0.500 L/s. Calculate the speed of the water (a) in the hose and (b) in the nozzle. Solution for (a) First, we solve for and note that the cross-sectional area is, yielding. Substituting known values and making appropriate unit conversions yields. Solution for (b) We could repeat this calculation to find the speed in the nozzle, but we will use the equation of continuity to give a somewhat different insight. Using the equation which states... substituting known values.
summary: This example demonstrates calculating water speed in both a hose and a nozzle using fluid dynamics principles.

header: Blood Flow in the Cardiovascular System
content: In many situations, including in the cardiovascular system, branching of the flow occurs. The blood is pumped from the heart into arteries that subdivide into smaller arteries (arterioles) which branch into very fine vessels called capillaries. In this situation, continuity of flow is maintained but it is the sum of the flow rates in each of the branches in any portion along the tube that is maintained. The equation of continuity in a more general form becomes where and are the number of branches in each of the sections along the tube.
summary: The cardiovascular system illustrates flow continuity, with blood branching into smaller vessels to maintain total flow.

header: Calculating Flow Speed and Vessel Diameter in the Aorta
content:
EXAMPLE 12.3
Calculating Flow Speed and Vessel Diameter: Branching in the Cardiovascular System
The aorta is the principal blood vessel through which blood leaves the heart in order to circulate around the body. (a) Calculate the average speed of the blood in the aorta if the flow rate is 5.0 L/min. The aorta has a radius of 10 mm. (b) Blood also flows through smaller blood vessels known as capillaries. When the rate of blood flow in the aorta is 5.0 L/min, the speed of blood in the capillaries is about 0.33 mm/s. Given that the average diameter of a capillary is, calculate the number of capillaries in the blood circulatory system.
summary: This example calculates the blood speed in the aorta and the number of capillaries based on known flow rates.

content: By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Explain the terms in Bernoulli’s equation. • Explain how Bernoulli’s equation is related to conservation of energy. • Explain how to derive Bernoulli’s principle from Bernoulli’s equation. • Calculate with Bernoulli’s principle. • List some applications of Bernoulli’s principle.
summary: This section details learning objectives related to Bernoulli's equation and its applications.

header: Bernoulli's Principle and Kinetic Energy Change
content: When a fluid flows into a narrower channel, its speed increases. That means its kinetic energy also increases. Where does that change in kinetic energy come from? The increased kinetic energy comes from the net work done on the fluid to push it into the channel and the work done on the fluid by the gravitational force, if the fluid changes vertical position. Recall the work-energy theorem...
summary: Bernoulli's principle explains how fluid speed and kinetic energy increase in narrower channels due to work done on the fluid.

header: Common Examples of Pressure Dropping in Fluids
content: There are a number of common examples of pressure dropping in rapidly-moving fluids. Shower curtains have a disagreeable habit of bulging into the shower stall when the shower is on. The high-velocity stream of water and air creates a region of lower pressure inside the shower, and standard atmospheric pressure on the other side. The pressure difference results in a net force inward pushing the curtain in.
summary: Pressure drops occur in rapidly-moving fluids, evidenced by shower curtains being pushed inward during a shower.

content: The relationship between pressure and velocity in fluids is described quantitatively by Bernoulli’s equation, named after its discoverer, the Swiss scientist Daniel Bernoulli (1700–1782). Bernoulli’s equation states that for an incompressible, frictionless fluid, the following sum is constant: where is the absolute pressure, is the fluid density, is the velocity of the fluid, is the height above some reference point, and is the acceleration due to gravity.
summary: Bernoulli's equation quantifies the relationship between pressure, velocity, and height in incompressible fluids.

header: Bernoulli’s Equation and Conservation of Energy
content: If we follow a small volume of fluid along its path, various quantities in the sum may change, but the total remains constant. Let the subscripts 1 and 2 refer to any two points along the path that the bit of fluid follows; Bernoulli’s equation becomes Bernoulli’s equation is a form of the conservation of energy principle.
summary: Bernoulli's equation represents a form of energy conservation for fluids, linking pressure and velocity changes.

content: Let us first consider the very simple situation where the fluid is static—that is, . Bernoulli’s equation in that case is We can further simplify the equation by taking (we can always choose some height to be zero... This equation tells us that, in static fluids, pressure increases with depth.
summary: In static fluids, Bernoulli's equation simplifies to show that pressure increases with depth.

content: Another important situation is one in which the fluid moves but its depth is constant—that is, . Under that condition, Bernoulli’s equation becomes… This equation is often called Bernoulli’s principle, indicating pressure drops as fluid speed increases.
summary: Bernoulli's principle describes how pressure drops in moving fluids at constant depth as velocity increases.

header: Calculating Pressure Drop in a Fluid Flow Example
content:
EXAMPLE 12.4
Calculating Pressure: Pressure Drops as a Fluid Speeds Up
In Example 12.2, we found that the speed of water in a hose increased from 1.96 m/s to 25.5 m/s going from the hose to the nozzle. Calculate the pressure in the hose, given that the absolute pressure in the nozzle is (atmospheric) and assuming level, frictionless flow.
summary: This example calculates the pressure drop as a fluid accelerates through a nozzle using Bernoulli's principle.

header: Application of Bernoulli’s Principle in Fluid Mechanics
content: There are a number of devices and situations in which fluid flows at a constant height and, thus, can be analyzed with Bernoulli’s principle. Entrainment devices have been in use since ancient times, particularly as pumps to raise water small heights, as in draining swamps, fields, or other low-lying areas. Some other devices that use the concept of entrainment are shown in Figure 12.5.
description: Figure 12.5 illustrates various entrainment devices that utilize increased fluid speed to create low-pressure areas, facilitating the movement of other fluids.
summary: Bernoulli's principle facilitates entrainment, guiding the use of devices for fluid movement by exploiting pressure differences.

header: Applications of Bernoulli's Principle in Lifting
content: The airplane wing is a beautiful example of Bernoulli’s principle in action. The wing is tilted upward at a small angle and the upper surface is longer, causing air to flow faster over it. The pressure on top of the wing is therefore reduced, creating a net upward force or lift.
summary: Bernoulli's principle applies to airplane wings, where differing airspeed creates lift through pressure differences.

content: Figure 12.7 shows two devices that measure fluid velocity based on Bernoulli’s principle. The manometer in Figure 12.7(a) is connected to two tubes that are small enough not to appreciably disturb the flow.
description: Figure 12.7 demonstrates how manometers and Pitot tubes measure fluid velocity based on changes in pressure.
summary: Devices like manometers utilize Bernoulli's principle to measure fluid velocity by assessing pressure differences.

header: Torricelli’s Law and Fluid Exit Speed
content:
EXAMPLE 12.5
Calculating Pressure: A Fire Hose Nozzle
Fire hoses used in major structure fires have inside diameters of 6.40 cm. Suppose such a hose carries a flow of 40.0 L/s starting at a gauge pressure of . The hose goes 10.0 m up a ladder to a nozzle having an inside diameter of 3.00 cm. Assuming negligible resistance, what is the pressure in the nozzle?
summary: This example applies Bernoulli's equation to calculate the pressure in a fire hose nozzle given flow and pressure parameters.

header: Power in Fluid Flow and Bernoulli's Equation
content: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy in any form is used or supplied. To see the relationship of power to fluid flow, consider Bernoulli’s equation…
summary: Power in fluid dynamics relates to energy transfer as expressed in Bernoulli's equation.

header: Calculating Power in a Moving Fluid Example
content:
EXAMPLE 12.6
Calculating Power in a Moving Fluid: Suppose the fire hose in the previous example is fed by a pump that receives water through a hose with a 6.40-cm diameter coming from a hydrant with a pressure of . What power does the pump supply to the water?
summary: This example calculates the power required by a pump supplying water to a fire hose under pressure.

header: Viscosity and Flow Dynamics - Learning Objectives
content: By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Define laminar flow and turbulent flow. • Explain what viscosity is. • Calculate flow and resistance with Poiseuille’s law. • Explain how pressure drops due to resistance.
summary: Learning objectives focus on viscosity, flow types, and how resistance impacts fluid flow.

content: When you pour yourself a glass of juice, the liquid flows freely and quickly. But when you pour syrup on your pancakes, that liquid flows slowly and sticks to the pitcher. The difference is fluid friction, both within the fluid itself and between the fluid and its surroundings. We call this property of fluids viscosity. Juice has low viscosity, whereas syrup has high viscosity.
summary: Viscosity is a measure of a fluid's resistance to flow, with lower viscosity fluids flowing more easily than those with higher viscosity.

content: The precise definition of viscosity is based on laminar, or nonturbulent, flow. Before we can define viscosity, then, we need to define laminar flow and turbulent flow. Laminar flow is characterized by the smooth flow of the fluid in layers that do not mix. Turbulent flow, or turbulence, is characterized by eddies and swirls that mix layers of fluid together.
summary: Laminar flow features smooth, layered movement of fluid, while turbulent flow involves chaotic mixing and eddies.

content: Turbulence has two main causes. First, any obstruction or sharp corner... This relationship is also reasonable; is like a lever arm, and the greater the lever arm, the less force that is needed. Fourth, is directly proportional to the coefficient of viscosity, . The greater the viscosity, the greater the force required.
summary: Turbulence and viscosity influence flow dynamics, with viscosity affecting resistance in fluids.

content: The resistance to laminar flow of an incompressible fluid having viscosity through a horizontal tube of uniform radius and length... This equation is called Poiseuille’s law for resistance after the French scientist J. L. Poiseuille (1799–1869), who derived it in an attempt to understand the flow of blood.
summary: Poiseuille's law quantifies resistance to laminar flow in tubes, highlighting factors like fluid viscosity and tube radius.

header: Blood Flow Regulation by Poiseuille's Law
content: The circulatory system provides many examples of Poiseuille’s law in action—with blood flow regulated by changes in vessel size and blood pressure. Blood vessels are not rigid but elastic. Adjustments to blood flow are primarily made by varying the size of the vessels, since the resistance is so sensitive to the radius.
summary: Poiseuille's law effectively explains how blood flow is regulated in the circulatory system by altering vessel dimensions.

header: Calculating Flow Rate with Artery Plaque Deposits
content:
EXAMPLE 12.7
Using Flow Rate: Plaque Deposits Reduce Blood Flow
Suppose the flow rate of blood in a coronary artery has been reduced to half its normal value by plaque deposits. By what factor has the radius of the artery been reduced, assuming no turbulence occurs?
summary: This example calculates the reduction in artery radius due to plaque buildup and its effect on blood flow rates.

header: Viscosity of Fluids at Various Temperatures
content: Table 12.1 lists the coefficients of viscosity for various fluids. Viscosity varies from one fluid to another by several orders of magnitude. As you might expect, the viscosities of gases are much less than those of liquids, and these viscosities are often temperature dependent.
description: Table 12.1 provides coefficients of viscosity for various fluids at different temperatures, illustrating the variability in fluid behavior.
summary: Viscosity varies greatly among fluids and is influenced by factors such as temperature and fluid type.

header: Flow and Pressure Drops in Water Supply Systems
content: You may have noticed that water pressure in your home might be lower than normal on hot summer days when there is more use. This pressure drop occurs in the water main before it reaches your home. Let us consider flow through the water main as illustrated in Figure 12.15.
description: Figure 12.15 depicts how pressure drops in a water main during periods of high usage, leading to reduced pressure at home.
summary: Pressure drops in water supply systems can occur due to high demand, affecting flow rates in homes.

header: Onset of Turbulence in Fluid Flow
content: Sometimes we can predict if flow will be laminar or turbulent. We know that flow in a very smooth tube or around a smooth, streamlined object will be laminar at low velocity. We also know that at high velocity, even flow in a smooth tube or around a smooth object will experience turbulence... Turbulence in the circulatory system is noisy and can sometimes be detected with a stethoscope.
summary: It is possible to predict flow conditions in a fluid system, with turbulence often detectable by characteristic sounds.

content: An indicator called the Reynolds number can reveal whether flow is laminar or turbulent. For flow in a tube of uniform diameter, the Reynolds number is defined as... For below about 2000, flow is laminar. For above about 3000, flow is turbulent.
summary: The Reynolds number quantifies flow characteristics, indicating whether conditions are laminar or turbulent.

content:
EXAMPLE 12.9
Is This Flow Laminar or Turbulent?
Calculate the Reynolds number for flow in the needle considered in Example 12.8 to verify the assumption that the flow is laminar.
summary: This example calculates the Reynolds number to confirm the expected flow conditions within a fluid system.

content:
Take-Home Experiment: Inhalation
Under the conditions of normal activity, an adult inhales about 1L of air during each inhalation. With the aid of a watch, determine the time for one of your own inhalations by timing several breaths and dividing the total length by the number of breaths.
summary: This home experiment assesses inhalation flow rate using personal timing to understand respiratory dynamics.

header: Understanding Diffusion Constants for Various Molecules
content: It can be shown that the average distance that a molecule travels is proportional to the square root of time: where stands for the root-mean-square distance and is the statistical average for the process. The quantity is the diffusion constant for the particular molecule in a specific medium. Table 12.2 lists representative values of for various substances, in units of .
description: Table 12.2 provides diffusion constants for various molecules. It lists the diffusion constants for substances including Hydrogen, Oxygen, Glucose, Hemoglobin, and DNA in different media.
summary: This section discusses the average distance molecules travel and introduces the concept of diffusion constants.

header: Random Walk of Molecules in Fluids
content: Note that gets progressively smaller for more massive molecules. This decrease is because the average molecular speed at a given temperature is inversely proportional to molecular mass. Thus the more massive molecules diffuse more slowly. Another interesting point is that for oxygen in air is much greater than for oxygen in water. In water, an oxygen molecule makes many more collisions in its random walk and is slowed considerably. In water, an oxygen molecule moves only about in 1 s. (Each molecule actually collides about times per second!). Finally, note that diffusion constants increase with temperature, because average molecular speed increases with temperature. This is because the average kinetic energy of molecules, , is proportional to absolute temperature.
description: This portion describes the principles underlying molecular diffusion in different media and how it varies with temperature and molecular mass.
summary: This section explains how molecular mass and temperature impact the rate of diffusion.

header: Calculating the Time for Glucose Diffusion
content: EXAMPLE 12.11 Calculating Diffusion: How Long Does Glucose Diffusion Take? Calculate the average time it takes a glucose molecule to move 1.0 cm in water. At 20°C and 1 atm Strategy We can use the expression for the average distance moved in time , and solve it for . All other quantities are known. Solution Solving for and substituting known values yields Discussion This is a remarkably long time for glucose to move a mere centimeter! For this reason, we stir sugar into water rather than waiting for it to diffuse. Because diffusion is typically very slow, its most important effects occur over small distances. For example, the cornea of the eye gets most of its oxygen by diffusion through the thin tear layer covering it.
summary: This example illustrates the calculation of time required for glucose to diffuse in water.

content: If you very carefully place a drop of food coloring in a still glass of water, it will slowly diffuse into the colorless surroundings until its concentration is the same everywhere. This type of diffusion is called free diffusion, because there are no barriers inhibiting it. Let us examine its direction and rate. Molecular motion is random in direction, and so simple chance dictates that more molecules will move out of a region of high concentration than into it. The net rate of diffusion is higher initially than after the process is partially completed.
description: This section discusses the principles of free diffusion and how it works in a medium without barriers.
summary: This section explains the concept of free diffusion and how it occurs in a medium.

content: The net rate of diffusion is proportional to the concentration difference. Many more molecules will leave a region of high concentration than will enter it from a region of low concentration. In fact, if the concentrations were the same, there would be no net movement. The net rate of diffusion is also proportional to the diffusion constant , which is determined experimentally. The farther a molecule can diffuse in a given time, the more likely it is to leave the region of high concentration. Many of the factors that affect the rate are hidden in the diffusion constant . For example, temperature and cohesive and adhesive forces all affect values of .
summary: This section discusses factors affecting the rate of diffusion, emphasizing concentration differences.

header: Role of Diffusion in Biological Exchanges
content: Diffusion is the dominant mechanism by which the exchange of nutrients and waste products occur between the blood and tissue, and between air and blood in the lungs. In the evolutionary process, as organisms became larger, they needed quicker methods of transportation than net diffusion, because of the larger distances involved in the transport, leading to the development of circulatory systems. Less sophisticated, single-celled organisms still rely totally on diffusion for the removal of waste products and the uptake of nutrients.
summary: This section emphasizes the importance of diffusion in nutrient and waste exchange in biological systems.

header: Diffusion Across Membranes: Osmosis and Dialysis
content: Some of the most interesting examples of diffusion occur through barriers that affect the rates of diffusion. For example, when you soak a swollen ankle in Epsom salt, water diffuses through your skin. Many substances regularly move through cell membranes; oxygen moves in, carbon dioxide moves out, nutrients go in, and wastes go out, for example. Because membranes are thin structures (typically to m across) diffusion rates through them can be high. Diffusion through membranes is an important method of transport.
description: This section highlights diffusion processes through membranes and their significance in transport.
summary: This section discusses how diffusion occurs across membranes, impacting substance transport.

content: Membranes are generally selectively permeable, or semipermeable. One type of semipermeable membrane has small pores that allow only small molecules to pass through. In other types of membranes, the molecules may actually dissolve in the membrane or react with molecules in the membrane while moving across. Membrane function, in fact, is the subject of much current research, involving not only physiology but also chemistry and physics.
summary: This section describes semipermeable membranes and their role in selective molecular transport.

content: Osmosis is the transport of water through a semipermeable membrane from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration. Osmosis is driven by the imbalance in water concentration. For example, water is more concentrated in your body than in Epsom salt. When you soak a swollen ankle in Epsom salt, the water moves out of your body into the lower-concentration region in the salt. Similarly, dialysis is the transport of any other molecule through a semipermeable membrane due to its concentration difference. Both osmosis and dialysis are used by the kidneys to cleanse the blood.
summary: This section explains osmosis, its mechanism, and its role in kidney function.

content: Osmosis can create a substantial pressure. Consider what happens if osmosis continues for some time, as illustrated in Figure 12.23. Water moves by osmosis from the left into the region on the right, where it is less concentrated, causing the solution on the right to rise. This movement will continue until the pressure created by the extra height of fluid on the right is large enough to stop further osmosis. This pressure is called back pressure. The back pressure that stops osmosis is also called the relative osmotic pressure if neither solution is pure water, and it is called the osmotic pressure if one solution is pure water.
description: This section describes the concept of osmotic pressure and how it affects fluid movement.
summary: This section discusses osmotic pressure and its role in the dynamics of fluid movement.

header: Practical Applications of Reverse Osmosis and Active Transport
content: Reverse osmosis and reverse dialysis (also called filtration) are processes that occur when back pressure is sufficient to reverse the normal direction of substances through membranes. Back pressure can be created naturally as on the right side of Figure 12.23. (A piston can also create this pressure.) Reverse osmosis can be used to desalinate water by simply forcing it through a membrane that will not pass salt. Similarly, reverse dialysis can be used to filter out any substance that a given membrane will not pass. One further example of the movement of substances through membranes deserves mention. We sometimes find that substances pass in the direction opposite to what we expect. Cypress tree roots, for example, extract pure water from salt water, although osmosis would move it in the opposite direction.
summary: This section explains reverse osmosis and active transport processes in substance movement.

content: This is not reverse osmosis, because there is no back pressure to cause it. What is happening is called active transport, a process in which a living membrane expends energy to move substances across it. Many living membranes move water and other substances by active transport. The kidneys, for example, not only use osmosis and dialysis—they also employ significant active transport to move substances into and out of blood. In fact, it is estimated that at least 25% of the body’s energy is expended on active transport of substances at the cellular level. The study of active transport carries us into the realms of microbiology, biophysics, and biochemistry and it is a fascinating application of the laws of nature to living structures.
summary: This section details active transport and its significance in cellular processes.

header: Glossary of Key Terms in Fluid Dynamics
content: active transport the process in which a living membrane expends energy to move substances across; Poiseuille’s law for resistance the resistance to laminar flow of an incompressible fluid in a tube:R=8ηl/πr4; Bernoulli’s equation the equation resulting from applying conservation of energy to an incompressible frictionless fluid:P+1/2pv2+pgh= constant; fluid dynamics the physics of fluids in motion.
summary: This section defines key terms relevant to fluid dynamics and related phenomena.

content: Flow rate is defined to be the volume flowing past a point in time , or where is volume and is time. The SI unit of volume is L. Another common unit is the liter (L), which is 1.0×10−3m3. Flow rate and velocity are related by A. For incompressible fluids, flow rate at various points is constant.
summary: This section introduces flow rate and discusses its relationship with velocity.

header: Bernoulli’s Equation: Conservation of Energy in Fluid Flow
content: Bernoulli’s equation states that the sum on each side of the following equation is constant, or the same at any two points in an incompressible frictionless fluid: P + 1/2pv^2 + pgh = constant.
