EH = (0.100 T)(20.0 cm/s)/(4.00 mm).

Discussion: This is the average voltage output. Instantaneous voltage varies with pulsating blood flow. The voltage is small in this type of measurement.
summary: This example demonstrates how to calculate Hall emf in a blood flow measurement using the principles of the Hall effect.

header: Magnetic Force on Current-Carrying Conductors: Basics
content:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:
• Describe the effects of a magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor.
• Calculate the magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor.
Because charges ordinarily cannot escape a conductor, the magnetic force on charges moving in a conductor is transmitted to the conductor itself.
description: Figure 22.29 shows the magnetic force acting on a current-carrying wire.
summary: This section introduces the effects of magnetic forces on current-carrying conductors.

header: Calculating Magnetic Force on a Current-Carrying Wire
content:
We can derive an expression for the magnetic force on a current by taking a sum of the magnetic forces on individual charges. The force on an individual charge moving at the drift velocity is given by F = qvB. Taking B to be uniform over a length of wire and zero elsewhere, the total magnetic force on the wire is then F = nql, where q is the number of charge carriers in the section of wire of length l. Now, n = q/V, where q is the number of charge carriers per unit volume and V is the volume of wire in the field. Noting that A = l, we gather terms to find: 
F = qIlB, 
where I is current and B is magnetic field strength.
description: Figure 22.30 shows the derivation of the magnetic force acting on a current-carrying wire.
summary: This section derives the formula for calculating the magnetic force acting on a current-carrying wire.

header: Example: Calculating Magnetic Force on a Current-Carrying Wire
content:
EXAMPLE 22.4 Calculating Magnetic Force on a Current-Carrying Wire: A Strong Magnetic Field Calculate the force on the wire shown in Figure 22.29, given F = IlB sin θ. 
Strategy: The force can be found with the given information considering that the angle θ is 90 degrees. 
Solution: Entering the given values into F = IlB gives the calculated force. 
Discussion: This large magnetic field creates a significant force on a small length of wire.
summary: This example illustrates how to calculate the magnetic force exerted on a current-carrying wire in a strong magnetic field.

header: Applications of Magnetic Forces on Current-Carrying Conductors
content: Magnetic force on current-carrying conductors is used to convert electric energy to work. Motors are a prime example—they employ loops of wire and are considered in the next section. Magnetohydrodynamics (MHD) is the technical name given to a clever application where magnetic force pumps fluids without moving mechanical parts.
description: Figure 22.31 illustrates the magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) system for pumping fluids using magnetic forces.
summary: This section discusses the applications of magnetic forces in electric motors and magnetohydrodynamic systems.

header: Torque on Current Loops: Motors and Meters
content:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:
• Describe how motors and meters work in terms of torque on a current loop.
• Calculate the torque on a current-carrying loop in a magnetic field.
Motors are the most common application of magnetic force on current-carrying wires. Motors have loops of wire in a magnetic field. When current is passed through the loops, the magnetic field exerts torque on the loops, which rotates a shaft. Electrical energy is converted to mechanical work in the process.
summary: This section introduces the principles of torque in motors and how they convert electrical energy to mechanical work.

header: Deriving Torque on a Current Loop
content: Let us examine the force on each segment of the loop in Figure 22.33 to find the torques produced about the axis of the vertical shaft. Torque is defined as τ = rF sinθ, where F is the force, r is the distance from the pivot that the force is applied, and θ is the angle between F and r. Torque is produced by forces on the side segments of the loop, while the top and bottom segments produce no torque due to being parallel to the shaft.
description: Figure 22.34 visually illustrates the resulting torque on a current-carrying loop.
summary: This section derives the concept of torque acting on current loops in motors.

content:
Finally, note that the area of the loop is A. The expression for the torque becomes: 
τ = nIA B sinθ,
where n is the number of turns, A is the area of each loop, B is the magnetic field strength, and θ is the angle between the axis of the loop and the magnetic field. This is the torque on a current-carrying loop in a uniform magnetic field.
summary: This section presents the final expression for torque in a current-carrying loop within a magnetic field.

header: Example: Calculating Maximum Torque on a Motor Loop
content:
EXAMPLE 22.5 Calculating Torque on a Current-Carrying Loop in a Strong Magnetic Field Find the maximum torque on a 100-turn square loop of wire of 10.0 cm on a side that carries 15.0 A of current in a 2.00-T field. 
Strategy: Maximum torque occurs when θ = 90°. 
Solution: For A = l², where l is the length of the loop side, the maximum torque can be calculated. 
Discussion: This torque is large enough to be useful in a motor.
summary: This example calculates maximum torque for a current-carrying motor loop in a strong magnetic field.

header: Continuing Motion in Motors: Brush Mechanism
content: The torque found in the preceding example is the maximum. As the coil rotates, the torque decreases to zero at θ = 0°. The torque then reverses its direction once the coil rotates past θ = 90°. This means that, unless we do something, the coil will oscillate back and forth about an equilibrium. To get the coil to continue rotating in the same direction, we can reverse the current as it passes through with automatic switches called brushes.
description: Figure 22.35 explains the functioning of brushes in motors, allowing for continuous rotation.
summary: This section describes how brushes in motors allow for continuous rotation of the coil.

header: Meters and Their Design Similarities to Motors
content: Meters, such as those in analog fuel gauges on a car, are another common application of magnetic torque on a current-carrying loop. The meter has its magnets shaped to limit the effect of magnetic field variation, making the torque proportional to current instead of angle. A linear spring exerts a counter-torque that balances the current-produced torque.
description: Figure 22.36 illustrates the internal structure and operation of a meter.
summary: This section compares meters to motors, explaining how magnetic torque is used to measure current.

header: Magnetic Fields Produced by Currents: Overview
content:
By the end of this section, you will be able to:
• Calculate the current that produces a magnetic field.
• Use the right hand rule 2 to determine the direction of current or the direction of magnetic field loops. How much current is needed to produce a significant magnetic field, perhaps as strong as the Earth’s field? Surveyors will tell you that overhead electric power lines create magnetic fields that interfere with their compass readings. Indeed, when Oersted discovered in 1820 that a current in a wire affected a compass needle, he was not dealing with extremely large currents.
summary: This section introduces the concept of magnetic fields generated by currents and their historical significance.

header: Direction of Magnetic Fields from Current-Carrying Wires
content:
The right hand rule 2 (RHR-2) emerges from exploring the direction of a magnetic field. Point the thumb in the direction of the current, and the fingers curl in the direction of the magnetic field loops created by it. The magnetic field strength (magnitude) produced by a long straight current-carrying wire is found by experiment to be: 
B = μ₀ I / (2πr), 
where μ₀ is the permeability of free space, and r is the shortest distance to the wire.
summary: This section describes the right hand rule for determining the direction of magnetic fields around current-carrying wires.

content:
Find the current in a long straight wire that would produce a magnetic field twice the strength of the Earth’s at a distance of 5.0 cm from the wire. The Earth’s field is about 50 μT, so we consider the magnetic field produced by the wire to be 100 μT. 
B = μ₀ I / (2πr). 
By rearranging the equation, we can calculate I.
summary: This section provides a practical example of calculating the current needed to achieve a specific magnetic field strength.

header: Exploring Magnetic Fields from Curved Wires: Ampere's Law
content:
Each segment of current produces a magnetic field like that of a long straight wire, and the total field of any shape current is the vector sum of the fields due to each segment. The formal statement of the direction and magnitude of the field due to each segment is called the Biot-Savart law. Integral calculus is needed to sum the field for an arbitrary shape current. This results in a more complete law, called Ampere’s law, which relates magnetic field and current in a general way. 
Making Connections: Relativity – Hearing all we do about Einstein, we sometimes get the impression that he invented relativity out of nothing. On the contrary, one of Einstein’s motivations was to solve difficulties in knowing how different observers see magnetic and electric fields.
summary: This section examines the mathematical foundations of magnetic fields produced by currents, introducing important laws.

header: Magnetic Field Strength near Circular Loops
content:
The magnetic field produced by a current-carrying loop is more complex. RHR-2 can be used to give the direction of the field near the loop, but mapping with compasses is needed for more detail. The magnetic field strength at the center of a circular loop can be expressed as: 
B = (μ₀I) / (2R), 
where R is the radius of the loop. It indicates a strong magnetic field at the center of the loop.
summary: This section defines the strength and direction of magnetic fields created by current-carrying loops.

header: Magnetic Field in a Solenoid: A Uniform Field
content:
A solenoid is a long coil of wire (with many turns or loops). The field inside a solenoid can be very uniform and also very strong. The magnetic field inside of a current-carrying solenoid can be expressed as: 
B = μ₀(n)I, 
where n is the number of loops per unit length of the solenoid. This can produce large uniform fields over a wide volume.
summary: This section discusses the properties of magnetic fields produced by solenoids and their applications.

header: Example: Calculating Internal Solenoid Field Strength
content:
EXAMPLE 22.7 Calculating Field Strength Inside a Solenoid: What is the field inside a 2.00-m-long solenoid that has 2000 loops and carries a 1600-A current? 
Strategy: To find the field strength, we will apply B = μ₀(n)I, calculating the number of loops per unit length. 
Solution: Substituting known values, we find that the strength achievable in practice is considerable, though challenging to maintain.
summary: This example calculates the magnetic field strength inside a solenoid based on its configurations and current.

content:
This section discusses how two parallel conductors carrying current interact magnetically. They will attract each other if the currents are in the same direction and repel if they are in opposite directions. The force per unit length between two parallel conductors can be calculated using the formula: 
F/L = (μ₀I₁I₂)/(2πd), 
where F is the force between conductors, d is the separation between conductors, and I₁ and I₂ are the currents through them.
summary: This section introduces the magnetic interactions between parallel conductors and provides a formula for calculating force between them.

content: The field outside the coils is nearly zero. This cutaway shows the magnetic field generated by the current in the solenoid. The magnetic field inside of a current-carrying solenoid is very uniform in direction and magnitude. Only near the ends does it begin to weaken and change direction. The field outside has similar complexities to flat loops and bar magnets, but the magnetic field strength inside a solenoid is simply given by the formula, where is the number of loops per unit length of the solenoid, with being the number of loops and the length. Note that is the field strength anywhere in the uniform region of the interior and not just at the center.
description: Figure showing the magnetic field generated by the current in the solenoid.
summary: The magnetic field inside a solenoid is uniform and only diminishes at the ends.

content: Example 22.7: Calculating Field Strength inside a Solenoid. What is the field inside a 2.00-m-long solenoid that has 2000 loops and carries a 1600-A current? Strategy: To find the field strength inside a solenoid, we use . First, we note the number of loops per unit length is . Solution: Substituting known values gives . Discussion: This is a large field strength that could be established over a large-diameter solenoid, such as in medical uses of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The very large current is an indication that the fields of this strength are not easily achieved, however.
summary: An example calculation illustrates how to determine the magnetic field inside a solenoid.

content: Higher currents can be achieved by using superconducting wires, although this is expensive. There is an upper limit to the current, since the superconducting state is disrupted by very large magnetic fields. There are interesting variations of the flat coil and solenoid. For example, the toroidal coil used to confine the reactive particles in tokamaks is much like a solenoid bent into a circle. The field inside a toroid is very strong but circular. Charged particles travel in circles, following the field lines, and collide with one another, perhaps inducing fusion. But the charged particles do not cross field lines and escape the toroid.
summary: Challenges associated with achieving high currents in solenoids, including the use of superconductors and toroidal coils.

header: Magnetic Field Strength and Material Influence
content: A whole range of coil shapes are used to produce all sorts of magnetic field shapes. Adding ferromagnetic materials produces greater field strengths and can have a significant effect on the shape of the field. Ferromagnetic materials tend to trap magnetic fields (the field lines bend into the ferromagnetic material, leaving weaker fields outside it) and are used as shields for devices that are adversely affected by magnetic fields, including the Earth’s magnetic field.
summary: Various coil shapes and the role of ferromagnetic materials in enhancing magnetic fields and shielding devices.

content: 22.10 Magnetic Force between Two Parallel Conductors. LEARNING OBJECTIVES. By the end of this section, you will be able to: Describe the effects of the magnetic force between two conductors. Calculate the force between two parallel conductors. You might expect that there are significant forces between current-carrying wires, since ordinary currents produce significant magnetic fields and these fields exert significant forces on ordinary currents.
summary: An introduction to the magnetic force experienced between current-carrying conductors.

content: But you might not expect that the force between wires is used to define the ampere. It might also surprise you to learn that this force has something to do with why large circuit breakers burn up when they attempt to interrupt large currents. The force between two long straight and parallel conductors separated by a distance can be found by applying what we have developed in preceding sections. The field produced by wire 1 and the force it exerts on wire 2 is described using the equation.
summary: Exploration of the significance of magnetic forces in defining the ampere and issues with circuit breakers.

content: Let us consider the field produced by wire 1 and the force it exerts on wire 2 (call the force ). The field due to at a distance is given to be... By Newton’s third law, the forces on the wires are equal in magnitude, and so we just write for the magnitude of . Since the wires are very long, it is convenient to think in terms of , the force per unit length.
summary: Relationships between the magnetic fields produced by wires and the forces exerted on them.

content: Substituting the expression for into the last equation and rearranging terms gives the relationship for the force per unit length between two parallel currents separated by a distance. The force is attractive if the currents are in the same direction and repulsive if they are in opposite directions. This force is responsible for the pinch effect in electric arcs and plasmas.
summary: Description of how the direction of current affects the force between parallel conductors.

content: The force exists whether the currents are in wires or not. In an electric arc, where currents are moving parallel to one another, there is an attraction that squeezes currents into a smaller tube.
summary: Explaining the pinch effect in both wires and electric arcs.

content: The operational definition of the ampere is based on the force between current-carrying wires. Note that for parallel wires separated by 1 meter with each carrying 1 ampere, the force per meter is exactly defined.
summary: Understanding how the ampere is defined through the interaction of current-carrying wires.

content: 22.11 More Applications of Magnetism. LEARNING OBJECTIVES. By the end of this section, you will be able to: Describe some applications of magnetism. Mass Spectrometry. The curved paths followed by charged particles in magnetic fields can be put to use.
summary: Introduction to how magnetism is utilized in mass spectrometry.

content: A charged particle moving perpendicular to a magnetic field travels in a circular path having a radius. It was noted that this relationship could be used to measure the mass of charged particles such as ions.
summary: Exploring the principle behind measuring mass using charged particles in magnetic fields.

content: A mass spectrometer is a device that measures such masses. Most mass spectrometers use magnetic fields for this purpose, although some of them have extremely sophisticated designs. Since there are five variables in the relationship, there are many possibilities.
summary: Overview of how mass spectrometers function and their design complexities.

content: However, if certain variables can be fixed, then the radius of the path is simply proportional to the mass of the charged particle. Let us examine one such mass spectrometer that has a relatively simple design. The process begins with an ion source, a device like an electron gun.
summary: Describing the initial phase of mass spectrometry using an ion source.

header: The Velocity Selector in Mass Spectrometry
content: The ion source gives ions their charge, accelerates them to some velocity, and directs a beam of them into the next stage of the spectrometer. This next region is a velocity selector that only allows particles with a particular value of to get through. The velocity selector has both an electric field and a magnetic field, perpendicular to one another.
summary: Examining the function of a velocity selector in a mass spectrometer.

header: Balance of Forces in the Velocity Selector
content: The forces on the ions in the selector produce effects that allow only selected ions to proceed. If the forces balance, then the electric force equals the magnetic force. Noting that cancels, we see that is the velocity particles must have to make it through the velocity selector.
summary: Discussion of the force balance concept in the velocity selector of mass spectrometers.

content: In the final region, there is only a uniform magnetic field, and so the charged particles move in circular arcs with radii proportional to particle mass. The paths also depend on charge, but since is in multiples of electron charges, it is easy to determine and discriminate between ions in different charge states.
summary: Outlining the last segment of the mass spectrometry process where particles are separated based on mass.

content: Mass spectrometry today is used extensively in chemistry and biology laboratories to identify chemical and biological substances according to their mass-to-charge ratios. In medicine, mass spectrometers are used to measure the concentration of isotopes used as tracers.
summary: Applications of mass spectrometry in various fields, including chemistry and medicine.

content: Usually, biological molecules such as proteins are very large, so they are broken down into smaller fragments before analyzing. Recently, large virus particles have been analyzed as a whole on mass spectrometers.
summary: Consideration of how large biological molecules are prepared for mass spectrometry analysis.

content: Sometimes a gas chromatograph or high-performance liquid chromatograph provides an initial separation of the large molecules, which are then input into the mass spectrometer.
summary: The role of chromatography in preparing samples for mass spectrometry.

content: Cathode Ray Tubes—CRTs—and the Like. What do non-flat-screen TVs, old computer monitors, x-ray machines, and the 2-mile-long Stanford Linear Accelerator have in common? All of them accelerate electrons, making them different versions of the electron gun. Many of these devices use magnetic fields to steer the accelerated electrons.
summary: Exploring the applications of CRTs and their use of magnetic fields to steer electrons.

content: Figure shows the construction of the type of cathode ray tube (CRT) found in some TVs, oscilloscopes, and old computer monitors. Two pairs of coils are used to steer the electrons, one vertically and the other horizontally, to their desired destination.
description: Figure illustrating the construction of a cathode ray tube.
summary: Details on how CRTs are constructed and how they steer electrons.

content: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is one of the most useful and rapidly growing medical imaging tools. It non-invasively produces two-dimensional and three-dimensional images of the body that provide important medical information with none of the hazards of x-rays.
summary: Introduction to magnetic resonance imaging as a non-invasive imaging technique.

content: MRI is based on an effect called nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) in which an externally applied magnetic field interacts with the nuclei of certain atoms, particularly those of hydrogen (protons). These nuclei possess their own small magnetic fields, similar to those of electrons and the current loops discussed earlier in this chapter.
summary: Explaining how MRI relies on nuclear magnetic resonance to produce images.

header: Nuclei Behavior in External Magnetic Fields
content: When placed in an external magnetic field, such nuclei experience a torque that pushes or aligns the nuclei into one of two new energy states—depending on the orientation of their spin (analogous to the N pole and S pole in a bar magnet).
summary: Describing how nuclei behave in a magnetic field and the concept of torque and energy states.

content: Transitions from the lower to higher energy state can be achieved by using an external radio frequency signal to “flip” the orientation of the small magnets. This is actually a quantum mechanical process. The specific frequency of the radio waves that are absorbed and reemitted depends sensitively on the type of nucleus, the chemical environment, and the external magnetic field strength.
summary: Understanding the transitions of energy states in nuclei through external signals.

content: Therefore, this is a resonance phenomenon in which nuclei in a magnetic field act like resonators that absorb and reemit only certain frequencies. Hence, the phenomenon is named nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). NMR has been used for more than 50 years as an analytical tool, with its formulation leading to a Nobel Prize in Physics.
summary: Defining NMR and its long-term significance as an analytical tool in science.

content: Over the past two decades, NMR has been developed to produce detailed images in a process now called magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), a name coined to avoid the use of the word 'nuclear' and the concomitant implication that nuclear radiation is involved.
summary: Tracing the evolution of NMR technology into the MRI we use today.

content: The 2003 Nobel Prize in Medicine went to P. Lauterbur and P. Mansfield for their work with MRI applications. The largest part of the MRI unit is a superconducting magnet that creates a magnetic field, typically between 1 and 2 T in strength, over a relatively large volume.
summary: Highlighting the contributions to MRI and the technology behind it.

content: MRI images can be both highly detailed and informative about structures and organ functions. It is helpful that normal and non-normal tissues respond differently for slight changes in the magnetic field. In most medical images, the protons, which are hydrogen nuclei, are imaged.
summary: Discussing the imaging capabilities of MRI and the focus on hydrogen nuclei.

content: Their location and density give a variety of medically useful information, such as organ function, the condition of tissue (as in the brain), and the shape of structures, such as vertebral disks and knee-joint surfaces.
summary: Expounding on the useful information that MRI outputs regarding various body structures.

content: MRI can also be used to follow the movement of certain ions across membranes, yielding information on active transport, osmosis, dialysis, and other phenomena. With excellent spatial resolution, MRI can provide information about tumors, strokes, shoulder injuries, infections, etc.
summary: Exploring the diverse diagnostic capabilities of MRI beyond just imaging.

content: An image requires position information as well as the density of a nuclear type (usually protons). By varying the magnetic field slightly over the volume to be imaged, the resonant frequency of the protons is made to vary with position.
summary: Explanation of the techniques used to acquire detailed images in MRI.

content: Broadcast radio frequencies are swept over an appropriate range and nuclei absorb and reemit them only if the nuclei are in a magnetic field with the correct strength. The imaging receiver gathers information through the body almost point by point, building up a tissue map.
summary: Describing how radio frequencies are used to develop tissue maps during MRI scans.

content: The reception of reemitted radio waves as a function of frequency thus gives position information. These 'slices' or cross sections through the body are only several mm thick. The intensity of the reemitted radio waves is proportional to the concentration of the nuclear type being flipped, as well as information on the chemical environment in that area of the body.
summary: Understanding how MRI produces image slices and correlates signal intensity with nuclear density.

content: Various techniques are available for enhancing contrast in images and for obtaining more information. Scans called T1, T2, or proton density scans rely on different relaxation mechanisms of nuclei. Relaxation refers to the time it takes for the protons to return to equilibrium after the external field is turned off.
summary: Discussing contrast enhancement techniques and relaxation mechanisms in MRI imaging.

header: Comparative Effectiveness of MRI and X-rays
content: While MRI images are superior to x-rays for certain types of tissue and have none of the hazards of x-rays, they do not completely supplant x-ray images. MRI is less effective than x-rays for detecting breaks in bone, for example, and in imaging breast tissue, so the two diagnostic tools complement each other.
summary: Contrasting the strengths and limitations of MRI compared to traditional x-ray imaging.

content: MRI images are also expensive compared to simple x-ray images and tend to be used most often where they supply information not readily obtained from x rays. Another disadvantage of MRI is that the patient is totally enclosed with detectors close to the body for about 30 minutes or more, leading to claustrophobia.
summary: Addressing practical considerations regarding MRI usage and patient experience.

content: It is also difficult for the obese patient to be in the magnet tunnel. New 'open-MRI' machines are now available in which the magnet does not completely surround the patient.
summary: Emerging open-MRI technology aimed at improving patient comfort.

content: Over the last decade, the development of much faster scans, called 'functional MRI' (fMRI), has allowed us to map the functioning of various regions in the brain responsible for thought and motor control. This technique measures the change in blood flow for activities (thought, experiences, action) in the brain.
summary: Exploring advances in fMRI technology and its application in brain function mapping.

header: Oxygen Consumption Measurement in Brain Activity
content: The nerve cells increase their consumption of oxygen when active. Blood hemoglobin releases oxygen to active nerve cells and has somewhat different magnetic properties when oxygenated than when deoxygenated. With MRI, we can measure this and detect a blood oxygen-dependent signal.
summary: Investigating how fMRI tracks oxygen levels to assess brain activity.

content: Most of the brain scans today use fMRI. Other Medical Uses of Magnetic Fields. Currents in nerve cells and the heart create magnetic fields like any other currents. These can be measured but with some difficulty since their strengths are about to less than the Earth’s magnetic field.
summary: Observation of varied applications of MRI and how magnetic fields from biological systems are measured.

content: Recording of the heart’s magnetic field as it beats is called a magnetocardiogram (MCG), while measurements of the brain’s magnetic field is called a magnetoencephalogram (MEG). Both give information that differs from that obtained by measuring the electric fields of these organs (ECGs and EEGs), but they are not yet of sufficient importance to make these difficult measurements common.
summary: Exploring the functions and differences of MCG and MEG in medical diagnostics.

content: In both of these techniques, the sensors do not touch the body. MCG can be used in fetal studies and is probably more sensitive than echocardiography. MCG also looks at the heart’s electrical activity whose voltage output is too small to be recorded by surface electrodes as in EKG.
summary: Discussing the non-invasive nature of MCG and its sensitivity in fetal studies.

content: MCG has the potential of being a rapid scan for early diagnosis of cardiac ischemia (obstruction of blood flow to the heart) or problems with the fetus. MEG can be used to identify abnormal electrical discharges in the brain that produce weak magnetic signals.
summary: Identifying the diagnostic potentials of MCG and MEG in medical practice.

content: Therefore, it looks at brain activity, not just brain structure. It has been used for studies of Alzheimer’s disease and epilepsy. Advances in instrumentation to measure very small magnetic fields have allowed these two techniques to be used more in recent years. What is used is a sensor called a SQUID, for superconducting quantum interference device.
summary: Exploring technological advances in measuring small magnetic fields for medical diagnostics.

content: This operates at liquid helium temperatures and can measure magnetic fields thousands of times smaller than the Earth’s. Finally, there is a burgeoning market for magnetic cures in which magnets are applied in a variety of ways to the body, from magnetic bracelets to magnetic mattresses.

keywords.summary: Investigating the emerging market for magnetic therapy devices.

keywords.header: Claims and Efficacy of Magnetic Healing
keywords.content: The best that can be said for such practices is that they are apparently harmless, unless the magnets get close to the patient’s computer or magnetic storage disks. Claims are made for a broad spectrum of benefits from cleansing the blood to giving the patient more energy, but clinical studies have not verified these claims, nor is there an identifiable mechanism by which such benefits might occur.
keywords.summary: Critically analyzing the medical claims associated with magnetic healing practices.

keywords.content: PHET EXPLORATIONS. Magnet and Compass. Ever wonder how a compass worked to point you to the Arctic? Explore the interactions between a compass and bar magnet, and then add the Earth and find the surprising answer! Vary the magnet's strength, and see how things change both inside and outside. Use the field meter to measure how the magnetic field changes.
keywords.summary: Engaging with a simulation that explores compass and magnet interactions.

keywords.content: Glossary. Ampere’s law the physical law that states that the magnetic field around an electric current is proportional to the current; each segment of current produces a magnetic field like that of a long straight wire, and the total field of any shape current is the vector sum of the fields due to each segment. B-field another term for magnetic field. Biot-Savart law a physical law that describes the magnetic field generated by an electric current in terms of a specific equation. Curie temperature the temperature above which a ferromagnetic material cannot be magnetized.
keywords.summary: Definition of key terms related to magnetism and electromagnetic theory.

keywords.content: domains regions within a material that behave like small bar magnets. electromagnet an object that is temporarily magnetic when an electrical current is passed through it. electromagnetism the use of electrical currents to induce magnetism. ferromagnetic materials, such as iron, cobalt, nickel, and gadolinium, that exhibit strong magnetic effects.
keywords.summary: Continued definitions of terms relevant to electromagnetism and magnetism.

keywords.content: gauss G, the unit of the magnetic field strength; Hall effect the creation of voltage across a current-carrying conductor by a magnetic field. Hall emf the electromotive force created by a current-carrying conductor by a magnetic field, in which case the Hall emf is created for a conductor of width through which charges move at a speed.
keywords.summary: Defining more specific terms associated with the measurement of magnetic fields.

keywords.content: Lorentz force the force on a charge moving in a magnetic field; magnetic field the representation of magnetic forces. magnetic field lines the pictorial representation of the strength and the direction of a magnetic field; magnetic field strength (magnitude) produced by a long straight current-carrying wire defined as where is the current, is the shortest distance to the wire, and is the permeability of free space.
keywords.summary: Concluding the glossary with definitions around the Lorentz force and magnetic field concepts.

keywords.content: Magnetism is a subject that includes the properties of magnets, the effect of the magnetic force on moving charges and currents, and the creation of magnetic fields by currents. There are two types of magnetic poles—north and south—with like poles repelling and unlike poles attracting. Electric field lines and magnetic field lines share some similarities but also differ significantly in their behavior.
keywords.summary: A thorough summary encapsulating the core concepts of magnetism.

header: Current and Equilibrium in Magnetic Fields
content: (a) What current must flow in the upper wire, neglecting the Earth’s field? (b) What is the smallest current if the Earth’s field is parallel to the ground and is not neglected? (c) Is the supported wire in a stable or unstable equilibrium if displaced vertically? If displaced horizontally?
summary: Discussion on currents and equilibrium states in a magnetic field.

header: Unreasonable Results: Magnetic Force on a Baseball
content: (a) Find the charge on a baseball, thrown at 35.0 m/s perpendicular to the Earth’s field, that experiences a 1.00-N magnetic force. (b) What is unreasonable about this result? (c) Which assumption or premise is responsible?
summary: Analysis of the charge needed on a baseball to produce a specified magnetic force.

header: Unreasonable Results: Helium Atom in Magnetic Field
content: (a) A charged particle having mass (that of a helium atom) moving at 1.50-T magnetic field travels in orbiting in a magnetic field with a circular path of radius 16.0 mm. (b) What is unreasonable about this result? (c) Which assumptions are responsible?
summary: Examination of unreasonable outcomes concerning a helium atom's behavior in a magnetic field.

content: A cyclotron accelerates charged particles as shown in Figure 22.61. Using the results of the previous problem, calculate the frequency of the accelerating voltage needed for a proton in a 1.20-T field.
description: Figure 22.61 illustrates a cyclotron design for accelerating charged particles.
summary: Calculation of frequency required for proton acceleration in a cyclotron.

content: (a) A 0.140-kg baseball, pitched at 40.0 m/s horizontally and perpendicular to the Earth’s horizontal field, has a 100-nC charge on it. What distance is it deflected from its path by the magnetic force, after traveling 30.0 m horizontally? (b) Would you suggest this as a secret technique for a pitcher to throw curve balls?
summary: Determining the deflection distance of a charged baseball in a magnetic field.

content: (a) What is the direction of the force on a wire carrying a current due east in a location where the Earth’s field is due north? (b) Calculate the force per meter if the wire carries 20.0 A and the field strength is needed. (c) What diameter copper wire would have its weight supported by this force? (d) Calculate the resistance per meter and the voltage per meter needed.
summary: Exploration of forces, resistance, and voltage for a wire in a magnetic field.

header: Construct Your Own Problem: Mass Separator
content: Consider a mass separator that applies a magnetic field perpendicular to the velocity of ions and separates the ions based on the radius of curvature of their paths in the field. Construct a problem in which you calculate the magnetic field strength needed to separate two ions that differ in mass, but not charge, and have the same initial velocity.
summary: Designing a problem focused on separation of ions in a magnetic field.

header: Critical Thinking: Force on Electrons in Magnetic Field
content: A magnetic field in the positive x-direction exists. Three electrons pass through the origin, one at a time, each with a velocity of v. The first electron passes the origin along the x-axis, the second electron along the y-axis, and the third at an angle of θ with respect to both the y- and z-axes in the yz-plane. (a) What is the force acting on each electron? (b) What is the shape of the path of each electron? (c) When, if ever, will each electron return to the origin?
summary: Analysis of forces acting on electrons in a defined magnetic field.

header: Understanding Electromagnetic Induction: Introduction to Concepts
content: Nature’s displays of symmetry are beautiful and alluring. A butterfly’s wings exhibit an appealing symmetry in a complex system. The laws of physics display symmetries at the most basic level—these symmetries are a source of wonder and imply deeper meaning. Since we place a high value on symmetry, we look for it when we explore nature. The remarkable thing is that we find it. The hint of symmetry between electricity and magnetism found in the preceding chapter will be elaborated upon in this chapter. Specifically, we know that a current creates a magnetic field. If nature is symmetric here, then perhaps a magnetic field can create a current. The Hall effect is a voltage caused by a magnetic force. That voltage could drive a current. Historically, it was very shortly after Oersted discovered currents cause magnetic fields that other scientists asked the following question: Can magnetic fields cause currents? The answer was soon found by experiment to be yes. In 1831, some 12 years after Oersted’s discovery, the English scientist Michael Faraday (1791–1862) and the American scientist Joseph Henry (1797–1878) independently demonstrated that magnetic fields can produce currents. The basic process of generating emfs (electromotive force) and, hence, currents with magnetic fields is known as induction; this process is also called magnetic induction to distinguish it from charging by induction, which utilizes the Coulomb force. Today, currents induced by magnetic fields are essential to our technological society. The ubiquitous generator—found in automobiles, on bicycles, in nuclear power plants, and so on—uses magnetism to generate current. Other devices that use magnetism to induce currents include pickup coils in electric guitars, transformers of every size, certain microphones, airport security gates, and damping mechanisms on sensitive chemical balances. Not so familiar perhaps, but important nevertheless, is that the behavior of AC circuits depends strongly on the effect of magnetic fields on currents.
description: N/A
summary: This section introduces the concepts of electromagnetic induction, highlighting the relationship between electricity and magnetism.

header: Learning Objectives for Induced Emf and Magnetic Flux
content: By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Calculate the flux of a uniform magnetic field through a loop of arbitrary orientation. • Describe methods to produce an electromotive force (emf) with a magnetic field or magnet and a loop of wire.
description: N/A
summary: The learning objectives outline the skills to be achieved related to calculating magnetic flux and generating emf.

header: Induced Emf Explained Through Faraday’s Experiment
content: The apparatus used by Faraday to demonstrate that magnetic fields can create currents is illustrated in Figure 23.3. When the switch is closed, a magnetic field is produced in the coil on the top part of the iron ring and transmitted to the coil on the bottom part of the ring. The galvanometer is used to detect any current induced in the coil on the bottom. It was found that each time the switch is closed, the galvanometer detects a current in one direction in the coil on the bottom. Each time the switch is opened, the galvanometer detects a current in the opposite direction. Interestingly, if the switch remains closed or open for any length of time, there is no current through the galvanometer. Closing and opening the switch induces the current. It is the change in magnetic field that creates the current. More basic than the current that flows is the emf that causes it. The current is a result of an emf induced by a changing magnetic field, whether or not there is a path for current to flow.
description: Figure 23.3 illustrates Faraday's apparatus demonstrating magnetic induction, showing the relationship between the switching action and induced emf in the galvanometer.
summary: Faraday's experiment demonstrates how changing magnetic fields can induce current in a coil.

header: Illustrating Induced Emf with Motion and Experiments
content: An experiment easily performed and often done in physics labs is illustrated in Figure 23.4. An emf is induced in the coil when a bar magnet is pushed in and out of it. Emfs of opposite signs are produced by motion in opposite directions, and the emfs are also reversed by reversing poles. The same results are produced if the coil is moved rather than the magnet—it is the relative motion that is important. The faster the motion, the greater the emf, and there is no emf when the magnet is stationary relative to the coil.
description: Figure 23.4 shows how the movement of a magnet relative to a coil produces induced emfs, emphasizing the importance of relative motion.
summary: Moving a magnet relative to a coil can create emfs, showcasing the principle of magnetic induction.

header: Comprehending Motion in Generators through Faraday’s Principle
content: The method of inducing an emf used in most electric generators is shown in Figure 23.5. A coil is rotated in a magnetic field, producing an alternating current emf, which depends on rotation rate and other factors that will be explored in later sections. Note that the generator is remarkably similar in construction to a motor (another symmetry). So we see that changing the magnitude or direction of a magnetic field produces an emf. Experiments revealed that there is a crucial quantity called the magnetic flux, 𝜓, given by the equation 𝜓 = B•A•cos(θ), where B is the magnetic field strength over an area A, at an angle θ with the perpendicular to the area as shown in Figure 23.6. Any change in magnetic flux induces an emf. This process is defined to be electromagnetic induction. Units of magnetic flux are weber. As seen in Figure 23.6, 𝜓, which is the component of B perpendicular to the area A. Thus magnetic flux is 𝜓 = B⊥A, the product of the area and the component of the magnetic field perpendicular to it.
description: Figure 23.5 shows the basic construction of a generator, where the rotation of a coil in a magnetic field induces emf, while Figure 23.6 explains magnetic flux in detail.
summary: This section discusses the principles behind electric generators and the significance of magnetic flux in inducing emf.

header: Understanding Faraday’s Law and its Implications
content: Faraday’s experiments showed that the emf induced by a change in magnetic flux depends on only a few factors. First, emf is directly proportional to the change in flux 𝜓. Second, emf is greatest when the change in time is smallest—that is, emf is inversely proportional to Δt. Finally, if a coil has N turns, an emf will be produced that is N times greater than for a single coil, so that emf is directly proportional to N. The equation for the emf induced by a change in magnetic flux is given by ε = - N (Δ𝜓/Δt). This relationship is known as Faraday’s law of induction. The units for emf are volts, as is usual. The minus sign in Faraday’s law of induction is very important. The minus means that the emf creates a current I and magnetic field B that oppose the change in flux 𝜓 —this is known as Lenz’s law. The direction (given by the minus sign) of the emf is so important that it is called Lenz’s law after the Russian Heinrich Lenz (1804–1865), who, like Faraday and Henry, independently investigated aspects of induction.
description: N/A
summary: Faraday’s Law quantitatively describes how induced emf is linked to changes in magnetic flux, emphasizing Lenz's law.

content: To use Lenz’s law to determine the directions of the induced magnetic fields, currents, and emfs: 1. Make a sketch of the situation for use in visualizing and recording directions. 2. Determine the direction of the magnetic field B. 3. Determine whether the flux is increasing or decreasing. 4. Now determine the direction of the induced magnetic field B. It opposes the change in flux by adding or subtracting from the original field. 5. Use RHR-2 to determine the direction of the induced current I that is responsible for the induced magnetic field B. 6. The direction (or polarity) of the induced emf will now drive a current in this direction and can be represented as current emerging from the positive terminal of the emf and returning to its negative terminal. For practice, apply these steps to the situations shown in Figure 23.7 and to others that are part of the following text material.
description: N/A
summary: A systematic approach for applying Lenz’s law to determine induced magnetic fields and currents is outlined.

content: There are many applications of Faraday’s Law of induction as we will explore in this chapter and others. At this juncture, let us mention several that have to do with data storage and magnetic fields. A very important application has to do with audio and video recording tapes. A plastic tape, coated with iron oxide, moves past a recording head. This recording head is basically a round iron ring about which is wrapped a coil of wire—an electromagnet. A signal in the form of a varying input current from a microphone or camera goes to the recording head. These signals (which are a function of the signal amplitude and frequency) produce varying magnetic fields at the recording head. As the tape moves past the recording head, the magnetic field orientations of the iron oxide molecules on the tape are changed thus recording the signal. In the playback mode, the magnetized tape is run past another head, similar in structure to the recording head. The different magnetic field orientations of the iron oxide molecules on the tape induces an emf in the coil of wire in the playback head. This signal then is sent to a loudspeaker or video player.
description: N/A
summary: This section explores real-world applications of electromagnetic induction in audio and video technology.

content: Similar principles apply to computer hard drives, except at a much faster rate. Here recordings are on a coated, spinning disk. Read heads historically were made to work on the principle of induction. However, the input information is carried in digital rather than analog form – a series of 0’s or 1’s are written upon the spinning hard drive. Today, most hard drive readout devices do not work on the principle of induction, but use a technique known as giant magnetoresistance. Another application of induction is found on the magnetic stripe on the back of your personal credit card as used at the grocery store or the ATM machine. This works on the same principle as the audio or video tape mentioned in the last paragraph in which a head reads personal information from your card.
description: N/A
summary: This section discusses further modern applications of electromagnetic induction in digital storage and credit cards.

header: Cochlear Implants and the Use of Induction
content: Another application of electromagnetic induction is when electrical signals need to be transmitted across a barrier. Consider the cochlear implant shown below. Sound is picked up by a microphone on the outside of the skull and is used to set up a varying magnetic field. A current is induced in a receiver secured in the bone beneath the skin and transmitted to electrodes in the inner ear. Electromagnetic induction can be used in other instances where electric signals need to be conveyed across various media.
description: N/A
summary: This section illustrates the application of electromagnetic induction in medical devices such as cochlear implants.

header: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation: A Contemporary Application
content: Another contemporary area of research in which electromagnetic induction is being successfully implemented (and with substantial potential) is transcranial magnetic simulation. A host of disorders, including depression and hallucinations can be traced to irregular localized electrical activity in the brain. In transcranial magnetic stimulation, a rapidly varying and very localized magnetic field is placed close to certain sites identified in the brain. Weak electric currents are induced in the identified sites and can result in recovery of electrical functioning in the brain tissue.
description: N/A
summary: This section discusses the innovative use of electromagnetic induction in treating brain disorders through transcranial magnetic stimulation.

header: Sleep Apnea Monitors Utilizing Electromagnetic Induction
content: Sleep apnea (“the cessation of breath”) affects both adults and infants (especially premature babies), and it may be a cause of sudden infant deaths (SID). In such individuals, breath can stop repeatedly during their sleep. A cessation of more than 20 seconds can be very dangerous. Stroke, heart failure, and tiredness are just some of the possible consequences for a person having sleep apnea. The concern in infants is the stopping of breath for these longer times. One type of monitor to alert parents when a child is not breathing uses electromagnetic induction. A wire wrapped around the infant’s chest has an alternating current running through it. The expansion and contraction of the infant’s chest as the infant breathes changes the area through the coil. A pickup coil located nearby has an alternating current induced in it due to the changing magnetic field of the initial wire. If the child stops breathing, there will be a change in the induced current, and so a parent can be alerted.
description: N/A
summary: This section describes the use of electromagnetic induction in monitoring children with sleep apnea.

header: Lenz’s Law and Conservation of Energy
content: Lenz’s law is a manifestation of the conservation of energy. The induced emf produces a current that opposes the change in flux because a change in flux means a change in energy. Energy can enter or leave, but not instantaneously. Lenz’s law is a consequence. As the change begins, the law says induction opposes and, thus, slows the change. In fact, if the induced emf were in the same direction as the change in flux, there would be a positive feedback that would give us free energy from no apparent source—conservation of energy would be violated.
description: N/A
summary: This section connects Lenz's law with the principle of conservation of energy to explain induced emf.

content: EXAMPLE 23.1 Calculating Emf: How Great Is the Induced Emf? Calculate the magnitude of the induced emf when the magnet in Figure 23.7(a) is thrust into the coil, given the following information: the single loop coil has a radius of 6.00 cm and the average value of (this is given, since the bar magnet’s field is complex) increases from 0.0500 T to 0.250 T in 0.100 s. Strategy To find the magnitude of emf, we use Faraday’s law of induction as stated by ε = - N (Δ𝜓/Δt), but without the minus sign that indicates direction: ε = N (Δ𝜓/Δt). Solution We are given that N = 1, but we must determine the change in flux before we can find emf. Since the area of the loop is fixed, we see that Δ𝜓 = B•A. Now A = π(0.060 m)^2 (area of loop) and since B changes from 0.0500 to 0.250 T.
description: N/A
summary: This example demonstrates the calculation of induced emf using Faraday’s law.

header: Exploring Motional Emf in Magnetic Fields
content: 23.3 Motional Emf LEARNING OBJECTIVES By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Calculate emf, force, magnetic field, and work due to the motion of an object in a magnetic field. As we have seen, any change in magnetic flux induces an emf opposing that change—a process known as induction. Motion is one of the major causes of induction. For example, a magnet moved toward a coil induces an emf, and a coil moved toward a magnet produces a similar emf. In this section, we concentrate on motion in a magnetic field that is stationary relative to the Earth, producing what is loosely called motional emf.
description: N/A
summary: This section focuses on the concept of motional emf and its relation to motion and magnetic fields.

header: Understanding the Hall Effect and Induction
content: Consider the situation shown in Figure 23.10. A rod is moved at a speed v along a pair of conducting rails separated by a distance d in a uniform magnetic field B. The rails are stationary relative to and are connected to a stationary resistor R. The resistor could be anything from a light bulb to a voltmeter. Consider the area enclosed by the moving rod, rails, and resistor. B is perpendicular to this area, and the area is increasing as the rod moves. Thus the magnetic flux enclosed by the rails, rod, and resistor is increasing. When flux changes, an emf is induced according to Faraday’s law of induction.
description: Figure 23.10 illustrates the induction of motional emf when a rod moves through a magnetic field, highlighting the increasing magnetic flux.
summary: This section describes motional emf induced when an object moves in a magnetic field, exemplified by the Hall effect.

content: To find the magnitude of emf induced along the moving rod, we use Faraday’s law of induction without the sign: ε = B•v•L. Here B and v are induced from motion. Note that the area swept out by the rod is changing, and thus we can find the overall effect when accounting for area A (A = d•L). When considering this effect in practice, for example in the case of the Tethered Satellite experiment, the induced emf could have been significant for currents passing through a conductor.
description: Figure 23.11 depicts the Tethered Satellite experiment where induced emf could convert kinetic energy into electrical energy.
summary: The section elaborates on the method for calculating induced motional emf due to the motion of an object in a magnetic field.

header: Analyzing Eddy Currents and Their Applications
content: Eddy Currents and Magnetic Damping LEARNING OBJECTIVES By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Explain the magnitude and direction of an induced eddy current, and the effect this will have on the object it is induced in. • Describe several applications of magnetic damping. Eddy currents are induced when a conductor moves in a magnetic field or when a magnetic field moves relative to a conductor. If motional emf can cause a current loop in the conductor, we refer to that current as an eddy current. Eddy currents can produce significant drag, called magnetic damping, on the motion involved.
description: N/A
summary: This section provides an overview of eddy currents, their formation, and effects like magnetic damping.

content: Consider the apparatus shown in Figure 23.12, which swings a pendulum bob between the poles of a strong magnet. This is another favorite physics lab activity. If the bob is metal, there is significant drag on the bob as it enters and leaves the field, quickly damping the motion. If, however, the bob is a slotted metal plate, as shown in Figure 23.12(b), there is a much smaller effect due to the magnet. There is no discernible effect on a bob made of an insulator. Why is there drag in both directions, and are there any uses for magnetic drag?
description: Figure 23.12 shows a physics demonstration with a metal pendulum bob experiencing magnetic damping due to eddy currents.
summary: This section discusses experimental demonstrations of eddy currents and their role in magnetic damping.

content: Figure 23.13 shows what happens to the metal plate as it enters and leaves the magnetic field. In both cases, it experiences a force opposing its motion. As it enters from the left, flux increases, and so an eddy current is set up in the counterclockwise direction (Lenz’s law), as shown. Only the right-hand side of the current loop is in the field, so that there is an unopposed force on it to the left. When the metal plate is completely inside the field, there is no eddy current if the field is uniform, since the flux remains constant in this region. But when the plate leaves the field on the right, flux decreases, causing an eddy current in the clockwise direction that experiences a force to the left, further slowing the motion.
description: Figure 23.13 illustrates the interaction of an eddy current induced in a plate moving in and out of a magnetic field.
summary: This segment explains the dynamics of eddy currents as they oppose the motion of an object in varying magnetic fields.

header: Eddy Currents in Slotted Plates and Insulators
content: When a slotted metal plate enters the field, as shown in Figure 23.14, an emf is induced by the change in flux, but it is less effective because the slots limit the size of the current loops. Moreover, adjacent loops have currents in opposite directions, and their effects cancel. When an insulating material is used, the eddy current is extremely small, and so magnetic damping on insulators is negligible. If eddy currents are to be avoided in conductors, then they can be slotted or constructed of thin layers of conducting material separated by insulating sheets.
description: Figure 23.14 depicts the impact of slots in a metal plate on the effectiveness of induced eddy currents.
summary: This section investigates how the structure of a conductor affects eddy current induction and magnetic damping.

content: One use of magnetic damping is found in sensitive laboratory balances. To have maximum sensitivity and accuracy, the balance must be as friction-free as possible. But if it is friction-free, then it will oscillate for a very long time. Magnetic damping is a simple and ideal solution. With magnetic damping, drag is proportional to speed and becomes zero at zero velocity. Thus the oscillations are quickly damped, after which the damping force disappears, allowing the balance to be very sensitive. In most balances, magnetic damping is accomplished with a conducting disc that rotates in a fixed field.
description: N/A
summary: This section outlines the practical applications of magnetic damping in enhancing the sensitivity of laboratory balances.

header: Magnetic Damping in Recycling and Separation
content: Since eddy currents and magnetic damping occur only in conductors, recycling centers can use magnets to separate metals from other materials. Trash is dumped in batches down a ramp, beneath which lies a powerful magnet. Conductors in the trash are slowed by magnetic damping while nonmetals in the trash move on, separating from the metals. A magnet can separate out the ferromagnetic materials alone by acting on stationary trash.
description: N/A
summary: This section discusses the role of magnetic damping in the separation of materials during recycling processes.

header: Applications of Eddy Currents in Detection Technologies
content: Other major applications of eddy currents are in metal detectors and braking systems in trains and roller coasters. Portable metal detectors consist of a primary coil carrying an alternating current and a secondary coil in which a current is induced. An eddy current will be induced in a piece of metal close to the detector which will cause a change in the induced current within the secondary coil, leading to some sort of signal like a shrill noise. Braking using eddy currents is safer because factors such as rain do not affect the braking and the braking is smoother. However, eddy currents cannot bring the motion to a complete stop, since the force produced decreases with speed. Thus, speed can be reduced from say 20 m/s to 5 m/s, but another form of braking is needed to completely stop the vehicle.
description: N/A
summary: This section highlights various applications of eddy currents in detection technologies and transport safety.

content: Electric generators induce an emf by rotating a coil in a magnetic field, as briefly discussed in Induced Emf and Magnetic Flux. We will now explore generators in more detail. Consider the following example: EXAMPLE 23.3 Calculating the Emf Induced in a Generator Coil. The generator coil shown in Figure 23.19 is rotated through one-fourth of a revolution (from to ) in 15.0 ms. The 200-turn circular coil has a 5.00 cm radius and is in a uniform 1.25 T magnetic field. What is the average emf induced?
description: Figure 23.19 illustrates the generator coil's construction, which plays a crucial role in emf generation.
summary: This section describes the principle of electric generators and presents an example to calculate induced emf.

header: Average Induced Emf Calculation in Generators
content: To find the average emf induced over a time t = 15.0 ms, we can specify that: ε = ∆𝜓/∆t. We know that and A = π(0.050 m)^2 since it was given that goes from = 0 to . Thus, substituting our values allows us to understand the variation in induced emf during the operation.
description: N/A
summary: This segment focuses on calculating the average induced emf in a generator through a practical example.

header: Understanding Maximum Induced Emf in Generators
content: EXAMPLE 23.4 Calculating the Maximum Emf of a Generator. Calculate the maximum emf, ε_peak, of the generator discussed in Example 23.3. Strategy Once the angular velocity (ω) is determined, ε_peak can be used to find ε. All other quantities are known. Solution Angular velocity is defined to be the change in angle per unit time: ω = Δθ/Δt. One-fourth of a revolution is radians, and the time is 0.0150 s; thus, substituting this value gives a calculated figure.
description: N/A
summary: This example demonstrates how to calculate the maximum emf of a generator based on angular velocity.

header: The Practical Usage of Electric Generators
content: The maximum emf ε_peak calculated is greater than the average emf found previously, as it should be. In real life, electric generators look a lot different than the figures in this section, but the principles are the same. The source of mechanical energy that turns the coil can be falling water (hydropower), steam produced by the burning of fossil fuels, or the kinetic energy of wind. Figure 23.23 shows a cutaway view of a steam turbine; steam moves over the blades connected to the shaft, which rotates the coil within the generator.
description: Figure 23.23 provides a cutaway view of a steam turbine, illustrating how mechanical energy is converted into electrical energy.
summary: This section discusses the diverse sources of mechanical energy used to power electric generators in practical applications.

content: It has been noted that motors and generators are very similar. Generators convert mechanical energy into electrical energy, whereas motors convert electrical energy into mechanical energy. Furthermore, motors and generators have the same construction. When the coil of a motor is turned, magnetic flux changes, and an emf (consistent with Faraday’s law of induction) is induced. The motor thus acts as a generator whenever its coil rotates. Lenz’s law tells us the emf opposes any change, so that the input emf that powers the motor will be opposed by the motor’s self-generated emf, called the back emf of the motor. The coil of a DC motor is represented as a resistor in this schematic. The back emf is represented as a variable emf that opposes the one driving the motor. Back emf is zero when the motor is not turning, and it increases proportionally to the motor’s angular velocity.
description: N/A
summary: This section describes the concept of back emf in motors and its relationship with electrical energy conversion.

header: Understanding the Dynamics of Back Emf
content: Back emf is the generator output of a motor, and so it is proportional to the motor’s angular velocity. It is zero when the motor is first turned on, meaning that the coil receives the full driving voltage and the motor draws maximum current when it is on but not turning. As the motor turns faster and faster, the back emf grows, always opposing the driving emf, reducing the voltage across the coil and the amount of current it draws. This effect is noticeable in a number of situations.
description: N/A
summary: This section outlines how back emf affects the performance and current draw of an electric motor.

content: Transformers do what their name implies—they transform voltages from one value to another (The term voltage is used rather than emf, because transformers have internal resistance). For example, many cell phones, laptops, video games, and power tools and small appliances have a transformer built into their plug-in unit. Transformers are also used at several points in the power distribution systems. Power is sent long distances at high voltages, because less current is required for a given amount of power, and this means less line loss, as was discussed previously. But high voltages pose greater hazards, so that transformers are employed to produce lower voltage at the user’s location.
description: N/A
summary: This section covers the fundamental purpose of transformers in voltage transformation and power distribution.

header: Construction and Operation of a Transformer
content: The type of transformer considered in this text is based on Faraday’s law of induction and is very similar in construction to the apparatus Faraday used to demonstrate magnetic fields could cause currents. The two coils are called the primary and secondary coils. In normal use, the input voltage is placed on the primary, and the secondary produces the transformed output voltage. Not only does the iron core trap the magnetic field created by the primary coil, its magnetization increases the field strength. Since the input voltage is AC, a time-varying magnetic flux is sent to the secondary, inducing its AC output voltage.
description: N/A
summary: This section details the construction and operational principles of transformers based on Faraday's law of induction.

content: For the simple transformer, the output voltage depends almost entirely on the input voltage and the ratio of the number of loops in the primary and secondary coils. Faraday’s law of induction for the secondary coil gives its induced output voltage to be ε_secondary = N_secondary (Δ𝜓/Δt). The output voltage equals the induced emf (ε), provided coil resistance is small. The input primary voltage is also related to changing flux by ε_primary = N_primary (Δ𝜓/Δt). Assuming negligible coil resistance, Kirchhoff’s loop rule tells us that the induced emf exactly equals the input voltage. Taking the ratio of these last two equations yields a useful relationship known as the transformer equation.
description: N/A
summary: This section explores the relationships governing transformer voltages as dictated by Faraday's law.

content: Assuming resistance is negligible, the electrical power output of a transformer equals its input (P_out = P_in). This is nearly true in practice as transformer efficiency often exceeds 99%. Equating the power input and output yields P_primary = P_secondary, where P = VI. Rearranging terms and combining with the transformer equation yields a relationship between the output and input currents of a transformer.
description: N/A
summary: This section presents calculations regarding transformer characteristics focusing on power output and input.

content: EXAMPLE 23.5 Calculating Characteristics of a Step-Up Transformer. A portable x-ray unit has a step-up transformer, the 120 V input of which is transformed to the 100 kV output needed by the x-ray tube. The primary has 50 loops and draws a current of 10.00 A when in use. (a) What is the number of loops in the secondary? (b) Find the current output of the secondary.
description: N/A
summary: This example guides through the calculations related to a step-up transformer in terms of loop numbers and output current.

content: A battery charger meant for a series connection of ten nickel-cadmium batteries (total emf of 12.5 V DC) needs to have a 15.0 V output to charge the batteries. It uses a step-down transformer with a 200-loop primary and a 120 V input. (a) How many loops should there be in the secondary coil? (b) If the charging current is 16.0 A, what is the input current?
description: N/A
summary: This section involves calculations concerning a step-down transformer for charging batteries.

content: LEARNING OBJECTIVES By the end of this section, you will be able to: • Explain how various modern safety features in electric circuits work, with an emphasis on how induction is employed. Electricity has two hazards. A thermal hazard occurs when there is electrical overheating. A shock hazard occurs when electric current passes through a person. Both hazards have already been discussed. Here we will concentrate on systems and devices that prevent electrical hazards.
description: N/A
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to electrical safety systems and the associated hazards.

content: Figure 23.29 shows the schematic for a simple AC circuit with no safety features. This is not how power is distributed in practice. Modern household and industrial wiring requires the three-wire system, shown schematically in Figure 23.30, which has several safety features. First is the familiar circuit breaker (or fuse) to prevent thermal overload. Second, there is a protective case around the appliance, such as a toaster or refrigerator. The case’s safety feature is that it prevents a person from touching exposed wires and coming into electrical contact with the circuit, helping prevent shocks.
description: Figures 23.29 and 23.30 depict a simple AC circuit without safety features and a three-wire system that includes safety measures.
summary: This part explains the essential safety features in modern electrical systems, including the three-wire system and circuit breakers.

content: There are three connections to earth or ground shown in Figure 23.30. Recall that an earth/ground connection is a low-resistance path directly to the earth. The two earth/ground connections on the neutral wire force it to be at zero volts relative to the earth, giving the wire its name. This wire is therefore safe to touch even if its insulation, usually white, is missing. The neutral wire is the return path for the current to follow to complete the circuit. Furthermore, the two earth/ground connections supply an alternative path through the earth, a good conductor, to complete the circuit.
description: N/A
summary: This section discusses the importance of grounding connections in electrical systems to ensure safety.

content: The earth/ground connection closest to the power source could be at the generating plant, while the other is at the user’s location. The third earth/ground is to the case of the appliance, through the green earth/ground wire, forcing the case, too, to be at zero volts. The live or hot wire supplies voltage and current to operate the appliance.
description: N/A
summary: This section elaborates on the grounding mechanisms employed in electrical appliances for added safety.

content: A note on insulation color-coding: Insulating plastic is color-coded to identify live/hot, neutral and ground wires but these codes vary around the world. Live/hot wires may be brown, red, black, blue or grey. Neutral wire may be blue, black or white. Since the same color may be used for live/hot or neutral in different parts of the world, it is essential to determine the color code in your region. The only exception is the earth/ground wire which is often green but may be yellow or just bare wire. Striped coatings are sometimes used for the benefit of those who are colorblind.
description: N/A
summary: This section discusses the importance of insulation color-coding in identifying electric wires for safety.

header: Addressing Worn Insulation and Electrical Shocks
content: Worn insulation allows the live/hot wire to come into direct contact with the metal case of this appliance. Lacking an earth/ground connection, a severe shock is possible. This is particularly dangerous in the kitchen, where a good connection to earth/ground is available through water on the floor or a water faucet. With the earth/ground connection intact, the circuit breaker will trip, forcing repair of the appliance.
description: N/A
summary: This section explains the dangers of worn insulation on electrical appliances and the protective role of grounding.

header: Understanding Leakage Current and GFI Functionality
content: Electric currents can induce an emf on the case of an appliance. If grounded, the case voltage is kept near zero, but if the case is not grounded, a shock can occur. The ground fault interrupter (GFI) is a safety device found in updated kitchen and bathroom wiring that works based on electromagnetic induction. GFIs compare the currents in the live/hot and neutral wires. When live/hot and neutral currents are not equal, it is almost always because current in the neutral is less than in the live/hot wire. Then some of the current, again called a leakage current, is returning to the voltage source by a path other than through the neutral wire.
description: N/A
summary: This section explores the function of GFIs in preventing shocks through detection of leakage currents.

header: Using GFI Mechanics to Prevent Electrical Hazards
content: GFIs are usually set to interrupt the circuit if the leakage current is greater than 5 mA, the accepted maximum harmless shock. Even if the leakage current goes safely to earth/ground through an intact earth/ground wire, the GFI will trip, forcing repair of the leakage.
description: N/A
summary: This section explains how GFIs function to interrupt circuits in the presence of harmful leakage currents.

content: Another induction-based safety device is the isolation transformer. Most isolation transformers have equal input and output voltages. Their function is to put a large resistance between the original voltage source and the device being operated. This prevents a complete circuit between them, even in the circumstances shown.
description: N/A
summary: This section discusses isolation transformers and their applications in enhancing electrical safety.

header: Understanding Ground Fault Interrupters in Electrical Safety
content: If the case is grounded, the induced emf is kept near zero. A ground fault interrupter (GFI) is a safety device found in updated kitchen and bathroom wiring that works based on electromagnetic induction. GFIs compare the currents in the live/hot and neutral wires. When live/hot and neutral currents are not equal, it is almost always because current in the neutral is less than in the live/hot wire. Then some of the current, again called a leakage current, is returning to the voltage source by a path other than through the neutral wire. It is assumed that this path presents a hazard, such as shown in Figure 23.34. GFIs are usually set to interrupt the circuit if the leakage current is greater than 5 mA, the accepted maximum harmless shock. Even if the leakage current goes safely to earth/ground through an intact earth/ground wire, the GFI will trip, forcing repair of the leakage.
description: Figure 23.34: A ground fault interrupter (GFI) compares the currents in the live/hot and neutral wires and will trip if their difference exceeds a safe value. The leakage current here follows a hazardous path that could have been prevented by an intact earth/ground wire.
summary: This section explains the function and purpose of ground fault interrupters in preventing electrical hazards.

content: Figure 23.35 shows how a GFI works. If the currents in the live/hot and neutral wires are equal, then they induce equal and opposite emfs in the coil. If not, then the circuit breaker will trip.
description: Figure 23.35: A GFI compares currents by using both to induce an emf in the same coil. If the currents are equal, they will induce equal but opposite emfs.
summary: This chunk discusses the operational mechanics of ground fault interrupters through induction.

content: Another induction-based safety device is the isolation transformer, shown in Figure 23.36. Most isolation transformers have equal input and output voltages.
